parsed_target.game_explanation:
This three-player bargaining game revolves around private values for red and blue chips, with green as a known common-value chip ($0.50 to everyone). Bear values blue chips the most ($1.00) and red chips moderately ($0.60). Buffalo values blue highly ($0.70) and red weakly ($0.10). Goose values both red and blue very little ($0.10 each). All players know their own values, see everyone’s chip counts and trade history, but do not know others’ exact values; they infer motives from which bundles are offered or accepted. The key surplus comes from moving blue chips away from Goose (who barely values them) to Bear and Buffalo (who value them). Red chips are also more valuable in Bear’s hands than in Buffalo’s or Goose’s, but the blue surplus is bigger and clearer. Because greens are common value, they are safe, transparent currency-like objects players can use to pay for more or less valuable reds/blues without raising suspicion about their private values.

Strategically, Bear is the natural aggregator of blue chips and will want to buy blue from Goose (and possibly from Buffalo) using green and red. Buffalo will seek to buy blue cheaply and sell red, and may also try to sell blue if they infer Bear values blue more. Goose, with low values for both red and blue, is happy to give up these chips for green or other chips if the calculator shows even small positive gains. But Goose does not know that their red/blue are undervalued relative to others and so might accept trades that yield modest calculator gains even when these are very favorable to Bear or Buffalo.

Bargaining dynamics reflect uncertainty and signaling. If someone persistently tries to buy a specific color, others infer that color might be valuable to them and become tougher sellers, demanding more green or asking for the other non-green color in return. Because non-senders respond simultaneously and only one accepting recipient is chosen at random, senders face an additional risk: if they offer a clearly profitable trade for both other players, both may accept, and the sender loses control over which partner they trade with. This tends to push proposals toward being particularly attractive to a target type (e.g., the obviously weak holder like Goose) or just barely acceptable to one counterpart but not the other.

Over three rounds with each player proposing once per round, early turns will likely be exploratory, revealing how eager others are to trade certain colors. Later turns will exploit discovered arbitrage, especially moving blue from Goose to Bear and/or Buffalo. Because chip counts carry over across turns, a profitable early trade can reshape later bargaining power and options. The forecast path below follows a plausible sequence where Bear successfully consolidates blue from Goose using green and red, Buffalo manages to acquire some extra blue early, and Goose steadily exchanges low-valued blue/red for green or small positive expected gains, without fully recognizing others’ higher valuations.

rounds.round_explanation: In the first round, everyone starts with symmetric endowments (10 of each color) but asymmetric private values. No one knows others’ exact valuations. Proposals in this round are likely exploratory, testing who is willing to sell blue or red and for what. Because green is common value and transparent, it is a natural medium of exchange. Bear, who strongly values blue, will tentatively try to buy blue, ideally from the seemingly weaker counterpart (Goose) while using green and red as payment. Buffalo may also try to acquire blue while dumping low-valued red. Goose, not realizing how undervalued their blue is, will accept modest positive gains in green or better-valued colors whenever offered.

By round 1, trade history reveals a pattern: Goose has readily sold blue to both Buffalo and Bear at modest prices, indicating Goose’s low effective valuation for blue. Buffalo and Bear both now hold more blue and fewer red/green. Bear, having paid green for blue, is more green-poor but still blue-hungry given their high $1.00 value for blue. Buffalo has improved value by swapping low-valued red for moderately valued blue. Players now infer that blue is broadly desirable to others, which may make Goose slightly more cautious selling blue again, and may change Buffalo’s and Bear’s willingness to part with remaining green. Strategies shift from pure exploration toward exploiting the discovered arbitrage while adjusting for updated beliefs about others’ preferences.

Entering the final round, Bear has accumulated a large share of blue chips, mainly from Goose, using a mix of green and red. Buffalo captured some extra blue early but has been unable to profitably trade further given their limited green and others’ learned reluctance to sell blue cheaply. Goose has surrendered several blue chips and some red in exchange for green and small perceived gains, not fully realizing the opportunity cost. With only three proposal turns left, players focus on any remaining positive-surplus trades that are simple to evaluate. Bear may still try to squeeze a small extra blue gain from Goose or Buffalo if feasible, while Buffalo and Goose may look for last-minute small improvements with green as the clean numeraire. The remaining trades are likely incremental rather than transformational, because the biggest blue reallocations have already occurred.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players value RED at $1.00 and GREEN at the common value $0.50, but differ on BLUE: otter=$0.40, peacock=$0.50, cat=$0.70. This makes BLUE the key speculative asset, with cat the strongest BLUE buyer and otter the weakest. Everyone has symmetric initial endowments (10 of each color), so pure value-maximizing trade pushes BLUE from otter→peacock→cat and possibly RED from cat→others if people suspect others might value RED more. However, each player only knows their own valuations, so early proposals reveal information about how much they privately value colors. The biggest frictions are: (1) proposers cannot target a specific partner—any accepting player could be matched randomly—so offers must be acceptable to any potential recipient willing to click accept; (2) with only three rounds and one proposal per player per round, players may anchor on safe, clearly profitable 1-for-1 or 1-for-2 exchanges that obviously improve their own payoffs regardless of who accepts; (3) uncertainty about others’ BLUE valuations can cause cautious proposals early, followed by more aggressive extraction attempts once someone’s willingness to trade becomes clear. Rational responders accept any proposal that yields strictly positive surplus in their own values, since the calculator shows them their net gain or loss. Over the full game, we expect at least a few BLUE-to-RED or BLUE-to-GREEN trades that move BLUE toward cat and possibly some RED redistribution if others are miscalibrated about its common $1.00 value, but declining trades and failed attempts to exploit informational advantage will limit total surplus extracted.

rounds.round_explanation: In the opening round, no one has revealed anything about their private BLUE valuations. Proposers are likely to start with simple, safe-seeming trades that feel roughly fair in the public range (e.g., 1-for-1 across colors or small 2-for-1s favoring the proposer). Otter moves first and, as the lowest BLUE valuer, has a strong incentive to shed BLUE or GREEN for RED but doesn’t yet know others share her RED preference. Peacock, with moderate BLUE, may try to improve her mix slightly by fishing for BLUE from someone else at a small premium or by swapping GREEN for RED. Cat, as the highest BLUE valuer, would like to buy BLUE and sell either RED or GREEN, but at this early stage she may test the waters with conservative offers so as not to overpay if others also love BLUE.

After round 0, the trade history has already revealed important information. Otter traded away BLUE for RED, signaling that she values RED strictly more than BLUE. Cat eagerly accepted both trades involving BLUE, strongly suggesting she is a high BLUE valuer. Peacock was willing to give up GREEN for BLUE but not to pay the steeper GREEN price Cat later asked, suggesting Peacock’s BLUE valuation is moderate. Now players can update beliefs: otter knows at least one opponent likes BLUE more than she does; cat sees demand for BLUE from Peacock and supply from Otter; peacock infers that cat is the strongest BLUE buyer. In this second round, otter will try to further offload BLUE or perhaps GREEN at attractive terms; peacock may tentatively sell some BLUE to cat at a profit or buy cheap RED/GREEN from otter; cat, having revealed her strong BLUE appetite, must be more careful not to overpay as others try to exploit her.

By the final round, valuation patterns are fairly clear: cat is a strong BLUE buyer but has resisted very unfavorable terms; peacock has sold some BLUE for RED when profitable and bought BLUE for GREEN at roughly equal value; otter has repeatedly traded away BLUE for RED when the calculator showed a gain. Everyone anticipates this is the last chance to adjust portfolios, so bargaining becomes more opportunistic. Peacock acts first and can try one more moderate trade that someone might accept if it shows them a clear dollar gain. Otter, going second, may still try to dump remaining BLUE or slightly rebalance using GREEN if safe. Cat, as the final proposer with no continuation value, can only profit from trades that generate a strictly positive calculator surplus and that counterparties still find attractive; any offer that looks even slightly negative to others will simply be rejected since there’s no future relationship to preserve.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game features private values for red and blue chips and a common value for green chips. All players start with 10 of each color. Green is worth $0.50 to everyone and is therefore a neutral store of value; red and blue are where the gains from trade lie. Badger values both red and blue at $0.80, Camel values red at $0.60 and blue at $0.40, and Squid values red at $0.20 and blue at $0.60. This implies large potential surplus between Badger and Squid on red chips (Badger likes red a lot, Squid dislikes it) and moderate potential surplus between Badger and Camel on blue chips (Badger likes blue more). Camel is moderately between Badger and Squid on red, and lowest on blue. However, the players do not know each other’s values; they only see their own surplus figures per proposal and know the public range for values. Strategic uncertainty arises because any proposal must be acceptable to at least one opponent based on that opponent’s unseen valuations, and acceptances are simultaneous with random tie-breaking. Proposers will tend to start conservatively—offering trades that are at least weakly beneficial for themselves at any acceptance and that they hope give enough surplus to at least one counterpart. Over time, observing which offers are accepted or declined reveals partial information about others’ tastes (e.g., frequent acceptance of red-for-blue suggests higher value for the bought color). Because there are only three rounds and each player has one proposal per round, they face a trade-off between being generous enough to induce acceptance and being greedy to maximize surplus per successful trade. As an outside observer with full information, we can anticipate that mutually beneficial red-for-blue trades between Badger and Squid generate the largest surplus, while Camel more cautiously adjusts holdings using green as a flexible medium when direct red–blue swaps seem too risky. The forecasted game below follows this logic: Badger gradually tests the market to move toward more red and blue that it values highly, Squid seeks to offload low-value red and pick up blue, and Camel experiments with small, safe adjustments around red and green when possible. Not all value-increasing trades will actually occur, because the players do not see each other’s payoff tables and must infer them indirectly via acceptances and rejections.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of round 0, no one has information about others’ private valuations beyond the known ranges. A reasonable expectation is that proposals will be simple and relatively small, probing whether others are willing to trade particular colors. Badger, who values both red and blue highly, is especially interested in acquiring more of these colors without giving up too much value; Camel has middling preferences and may use green as a neutral chip to test interest; Squid strongly prefers blue over red and will be attracted to offers that let it move red out and blue in at a favorable rate. With full stacks intact, and three full rounds ahead, players can afford to decline unattractive offers and wait for better ones, so early proposals are likely to be modest, exploratory, and sometimes declined if they appear one-sided or confusing.

By the start of round 1, two informative trades have occurred. First, Badger successfully bought red from Camel using green, suggesting to Camel that Badger places relatively high value on red or lower value on green. Second, Squid’s offer to trade 4 red for 3 blue was accepted by Badger, revealing that both Badger and Squid see some benefit in swapping red and blue at that ratio, though perhaps for different reasons. Camel’s green-for-blue attempt was rejected by both others, hinting that neither Badger nor Squid finds exchanging green for blue at that rate attractive. Now the players update their tentative beliefs: Badger suspects others may be willing to part with red or blue if compensated, Camel becomes more cautious about overpaying with green, and Squid confirms that offloading red can work if the rate is favorable. With only two remaining rounds, players remain somewhat conservative but feel mild pressure to secure at least one or two more profitable trades.

Entering the final round, some structure in perceived preferences has emerged. Badger has repeatedly shown willingness to exchange green to acquire more red and blue, building a portfolio tilted toward the colors it values at $0.80 each. Camel’s proposals have been declined so far, so Camel may feel under pressure to achieve at least one accepted, modestly profitable trade, but must be wary of large net losses visible in the surplus calculator. Squid has achieved one big surplus trade by selling red for blue to Badger and one additional beneficial trade selling blue to Badger; Squid now holds relatively fewer red and more blue than at the start. With only three final proposals remaining (one per player), each proposer has a strong incentive to craft offers that are likely to be accepted, even if that means sharing more surplus with others, because unexecuted offers yield zero. This leads to slightly more generous, cleaner exchanges: Badger still tries to buy higher-valued colors for itself without giving up too much, Camel focuses on a safe red–green test, and Squid finishes by offering a trade that is good for Badger but not for Camel, banking on Badger as a likely partner.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game features two privately valued chip colors (RED and BLUE) and one common-value color (GREEN). Each player starts with 10 of each type. GREEN is worth $0.50 to everyone, so those chips serve as a natural, transparent medium of exchange and an obvious source of mutually beneficial trades: nobody will ever want to give up GREEN cheaply, and symmetric GREEN trades are pointless. The strategic action centers on RED and BLUE, where private values differ. Dog values RED very highly and BLUE very little relative to Butterfly; Butterfly strongly values BLUE and moderately values RED; Ram values both RED and BLUE low. Dog therefore wants to accumulate RED and shed BLUE, Butterfly wants to accumulate BLUE and may shed some RED, and Ram is a relatively weak bargainer who would like to trade any privately low-value colors for GREEN when possible. However, none of them know the others’ exact values, only public ranges, so they must infer demand from observed acceptances and refusals. Proposers have a surplus calculator that tells them, from their own perspective, how good or bad a drafted trade is, and responders see their own net change when evaluating a posted offer. Thus, no one will knowingly accept a strictly negative-value trade. Players will tend to propose trades that are positive for themselves in expectation but that they hope are at least weakly positive for some other player under plausible valuations. Because multiple acceptances are resolved randomly, a proposer cannot target a specific partner; they must design offers that are acceptable to at least one likely counterparty, while trying to avoid making the offer so generous that both others profit a lot at the proposer’s expense. Over three short rounds, the bargaining dynamics are exploratory: early offers are often conservative and information-gathering, and rejections help reveal where others’ reservation values likely lie. As the game progresses, players who have learned something (for example, that someone is eager for BLUE) may adjust their offers to capture more surplus, while those with few good outside options (like Ram) may accept small but positive gains in GREEN or in a relatively more valuable color to avoid ending with an inefficient portfolio.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of the game, everyone has symmetric holdings but asymmetric, unknown values. Round 0 is where players experiment to discover others’ interests. Dog, valuing RED highly, is reluctant to give much RED away without good compensation, and may tentatively probe demand for GREEN or BLUE. Butterfly will likely test the market for BLUE, perhaps offering some RED or GREEN to see who bites. Ram, with low private values on both RED and BLUE, is inclined to trade either of those away for GREEN if possible, but does not yet know how much others value GREEN versus the colored chips.

By the start of Round 1, Ram has revealed a willingness to give up RED and BLUE for GREEN, suggesting to others that Ram’s private value for GREEN is relatively high or that Ram has low values for the colored chips. Dog has given away some BLUE for GREEN, signaling that BLUE is relatively unimportant to Dog. Butterfly has traded RED for GREEN, hinting that RED may not be as valuable to Butterfly as GREEN. With this emerging information, Dog is likely to keep using BLUE as a funding source to buy GREEN or occasionally RED; Butterfly will search for profitable ways to turn GREEN or low-value RED into more BLUE; and Ram will try again to convert unwanted colored chips into GREEN, though now others are less inclined to give up GREEN cheaply because they have inferred its central role and common value.

Going into the final round, Dog has converted several low-value BLUE chips into GREEN, Butterfly has made only one modest gain earlier and is motivated to secure at least one more profitable trade, and Ram has learned that repeated attempts to buy GREEN with RED at a 1:1 rate are unattractive to others. Everyone now understands that GREEN is tightly held and costly to buy. Dog’s best remaining opportunities involve trading away more BLUE or, reluctantly, some GREEN if compensated with very valuable RED. Butterfly, who values BLUE the most, may try to entice either Dog or Ram to part with BLUE, but must offer enough GREEN or RED to make it acceptable given the history of rejections. Ram, having failed repeatedly to buy GREEN cheaply, may pivot to small, mutually beneficial color-for-color trades, but with only one more chance to propose, Ram’s bargaining power is limited. Many marginal or exploitative offers will now be declined because players recognize they are near the end and will not accept negative or tiny gains that do not justify degrading their chip mix.

parsed_target.game_explanation:
This three-player bargaining game features privately known values on red and blue chips and a common value on green chips. All players start with symmetric endowments (10 of each chip), but their incentive to trade differs because lion values blue chips the most and iguana values red chips the most, while alligator is intermediate on both. Specifically, lion has the strongest motive to acquire blue chips (worth $1.00 each to them, more than to anyone else) and can profitably trade away red chips (worth only $0.40 to them, compared to $0.80 for iguana). Iguana has the mirror-image motive: they most want red chips and can give up blue chips (worth only $0.40 to them, versus $1.00 to lion). Alligator values both red and blue moderately and green equally with others, so alligator is more likely to act as a cautious, opportunistic trader rather than the primary driver of large cross-player reallocations.

Because players only see their own values, each proposal implicitly signals which colors they like or dislike, but uncertainty remains about precise valuations and about who is the most eager buyer or seller for each chip type. Participants reason that any accepted offer must give the recipient a nonnegative surplus given their calculator, but they do not observe the other's underlying per-chip values. The randomization rule when multiple players accept introduces another twist: a sender cannot target a specific counterpart, so they must design trades that are acceptable to any potential counterparty who might plausibly accept, or else they risk accidental trades with the "wrong" partner.

In equilibrium-like reasoning, one expects large potential gains from trade between lion and iguana: lion should want to give red and receive blue, while iguana should want to give blue and receive red. Alligator can sometimes profit by buying undervalued colors from one player and reselling to another, but with only three rounds total and one proposal each per round, there is limited time to build complex multi-step arbitrage. Early proposals will tend to be relatively small, information-revealing offers, probing whether others are willing sellers of a given color. Later rounds are more likely to feature slightly larger, more targeted trades once players have inferred that some counterpart has a strong preference in the opposite direction.

Given the per-chip values, individually rational trade patterns include: lion exchanging red for blue with iguana; iguana exchanging blue for red with lion; and alligator trading red for blue with either side if the price ratio is favorable. Green is common-value at $0.50 and thus rarely traded except possibly as a neutral currency when players are experimenting. Uncertainty persists about exactly how desperate each player is for marginal chips, so some offers that are profitable to the proposer may be declined because the implied relative price is too unfavorable to the recipient. As the game proceeds, the public trade history (who offers what, and who accepts) helps each player update beliefs about others' preferences, and final-round behavior often reflects these updated inferences as players attempt to lock in any remaining profitable reallocation before the game ends.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of round 0 all players hold 10 of each chip type and know only their own chip values. No one yet knows who most prefers red versus blue, so proposals in this round are likely to be exploratory and modest in size. Alligator moves first and will plausibly test whether anyone is a willing counterparty to trade blue for red or vice versa at a favorable rate for them. Lion and iguana, as responders, will use alligator's initial offer to begin inferring whether alligator is relatively red-loving or blue-loving. Later in the round, lion and iguana each get a chance to propose, with strong incentives for lion to move toward acquiring blue and for iguana to acquire red, but they also face uncertainty about whether the other is indeed on the opposite side of the market and what relative price will be accepted.

By the start of round 1, lion and iguana have successfully traded once: lion gave up some red to gain blue, and iguana did the opposite. This confirms for both of them, and for alligator observing the history, that lion is a strong buyer of blue and seller of red, while iguana is a strong buyer of red and seller of blue. Alligator now has more information and may try to exploit a potential middleman position, designing a trade that improves their own portfolio without losing surplus. Lion, already having improved their blue holdings, may push for additional red-for-blue trades if favorable, but will be cautious about offers that give away too much value. Iguana, having tested market interest with their previous proposal, now better understands that lion is a key counterparty and may adjust prices or quantities to seek further gains.

Entering the final round, lion has increased their blue holdings at the cost of red, and iguana has increased their red holdings at the cost of blue, moving the allocation roughly in the efficiency-improving direction consistent with their valuations. Alligator has not traded yet and has observed a clear pattern that lion repeatedly pays red for blue and iguana repeatedly pays blue for red. However, the remaining opportunities may be marginal: lion and iguana are closer to their preferred portfolios, and they will now be more selective, avoiding trades that only slightly improve or even reduce their surplus. Because this is the last chance to trade, each player may either attempt one final mutually beneficial small trade or simply decline unattractive offers, accepting their current holdings. Proposals are therefore likely to be either finely tuned win-win offers or somewhat aggressive attempts that end up being rejected as too one-sided.

parsed_target.game_explanation:
This three-player bargaining game features privately valued red and blue chips and common-value green chips. Each player starts with an equal bundle (10 of each color), but the marginal values diverge: whale-red-86 and duck-orange-4971 both value red chips highly ($1.00) and blue chips moderately ($0.40 and $0.60, respectively), while dolphin-orange-3122 values blue chips highly ($0.80) and red chips less ($0.60). Green chips are worth $0.50 to everyone and thus provide a neutral, safe medium for trade. The players see only their own values but know the public ranges, and they have a surplus calculator that makes it transparent whether any given trade is individually profitable ex post. However, they must form beliefs about the others’ relative red vs. blue valuations based only on proposals and acceptances. 

Given the valuations, high-value directions for surplus creation are: (i) moving red chips from dolphin (lower red value) to whale or duck (higher red value), and (ii) moving blue chips from whale (lowest blue value) toward dolphin or duck (higher blue values). Green chips serve as a natural side payment or price anchor, because their value is identical across players, allowing partially efficient trades to be sweetened to make both sides strictly better off.

Strategically, proposers must design offers that are (a) strictly profitable to themselves per the calculator, (b) attractive enough to be accepted by at least one counterparty, and (c) not so generous that they leave surplus on the table. Because multiple acceptances are resolved randomly, proposers cannot fully target a specific counterparty and may sometimes want to design offers that are at least weakly acceptable to both other players, or, conversely, sufficiently tailored that only one is likely to accept. Over three short rounds with a fixed proposal order (whale, then dolphin, then duck in each round), early moves serve both to extract surplus and to signal private valuations. Later rounds can exploit updated beliefs: e.g., if dolphin observes consistent eagerness to acquire red from whale, dolphin can infer that red may be relatively more valuable to the others and adjust prices accordingly.

Across the forecasted game, we expect small, conservative trades in early turns as players probe others’ willingness to trade. Whale is naturally inclined to shed blue for red or green and to acquire red when possible, but must overcome uncertainty about how much the others value red. Dolphin, as the high-blue player, is inclined to sell red or green for blue and may use green as an enticement. Duck, who shares whale’s high red valuation but has a stronger blue valuation than whale, will often sit out unattractive trades and selectively buy red cheaply from dolphin or sell blue to whale when the price is favorable. Because each decision is myopic profit maximization with a clear calculator, we should see no player accepting a strictly negative-surplus trade, and the pattern of accepted trades will align with the direction of underlying comparative advantages in chip values, while still reflecting noisy, boundedly strategic beliefs about others’ payoffs.

rounds.round_explanation: In round 0, players have no history to infer one another’s private values. Proposers are likely to start with relatively simple, moderately favorable offers that reflect their own comparative advantages. Whale, moving first, will probably test the waters by offering lower-valued blue or modest amounts of red in exchange for green or red, while being careful not to give others a large risk-free gain. Dolphin, as the high-blue player, will be inclined to try to accumulate more blue cheaply, possibly selling red or green. Duck, moving last, will react to any revealed patterns—for example, any strong demand for red or blue—while still having limited information. The uncertainty over the others’ valuations and the random tie-breaking if both opponents accept will push proposals toward being at least somewhat attractive to both potential recipients, but without being so generous that the proposer sacrifices too much surplus.

By the start of round 1, a bit of information is revealed: whale was willing to trade away blue for green at an even rate, and dolphin accepted that, indicating that dolphin does not view green as much worse than blue at that margin. The failed dolphin proposal to buy blue using a relatively large amount of green suggests both whale and duck may value their blue more than the implied green price. Duck’s failed attempt to buy red with green hints that the others are not willing to part with red cheaply for green alone, which is consistent with red having relatively high private values. In round 1, we expect more targeted offers: whale may again try to convert blue into red or green but now with slightly different pricing; dolphin may re-balance the mix of green and perhaps red used to buy blue; and duck may either wait for better prices or propose trades that exploit whale’s apparent eagerness to move blue.

Going into the final round, the history suggests that both whale and duck are willing to trade red for blue at certain ratios, and that dolphin is very willing to trade red or green in order to gain blue, as long as the price is not too high in terms of green. Previous acceptances show: whale accepted paying 2 blue for 2 red earlier (through duck’s acceptance of whale’s offer), duck accepted 3 blue for 2 red from whale, and duck also sold 2 red for 2 blue to dolphin, signaling flexible attitudes toward the red–blue rate depending on context. With only one round left, each player focuses on extracting last bits of surplus from their current portfolios. Whale is still motivated to improve red holdings if the price in blue or green is favorable. Dolphin looks to add more high-value blue or possibly shed excess green. Duck, with good valuations for both red and blue, is cautious: they accept only trades that give them a clear net gain in value and are likely to reject marginal or slightly negative offers, especially since no future rounds remain to exploit any strategic signaling.

parsed_target.game_explanation:
This three-player bargaining game features privately known values for red and blue chips, with green chips being common-value at $0.50 for all. Everyone starts with 10 of each color. The key asymmetries: turtle values blue highest ($1.00) and red slightly less ($0.90); gorilla values red highest ($1.00) and blue somewhat ($0.70); zebra values both red and blue relatively low ($0.60 and $0.50). Green is perfectly liquid and symmetric, so it is a natural medium of exchange and baseline store of value.

Efficient trades move red chips from zebra (who values them at $0.60) and turtle (at $0.90) toward gorilla (at $1.00), and move blue chips from zebra (at $0.50) and gorilla (at $0.70) toward turtle (at $1.00). Zebra is the relatively low-value holder of both red and blue and is the most natural source of surplus: giving up colored chips in exchange for green is often profitable for zebra if prices are not too unfavorable. Gorilla wants to accumulate red, and turtle wants to accumulate blue. Since players only know their own values, they must infer others’ valuations from observed behavior and the known public ranges. The surplus calculator ensures they never knowingly accept a strictly negative deal, so trades will occur only when both sides see a nonnegative gain from their own perspective.

Because recipients are chosen randomly when multiple players accept, proposers have to consider both potential counterparty types and may post offers that are at least weakly attractive to more than one opponent. However, the tight value ranges make it plausible that proposers aim their trades at the counterpart they suspect values the opposite color more highly, especially targeting zebra as a willing seller of colored chips for green. Early in the game, players tentatively test the market with modest trades, learning who responds to which prices. As the final round approaches, there is no reason to save surplus for later, so players become more willing to propose aggressive, even somewhat exploitive trades if they think others are desperate to grab any small gain before the game ends. Overall, we expect a sequence of small, individually rational exchanges that move red toward gorilla, blue toward turtle, and green toward zebra, with some failed attempts where proposals are mispriced relative to others’ unknown values.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start, all players hold 10 of each chip color and know only their own private values plus common ranges. They have no direct information yet about others’ valuations, so round 0 serves as an exploratory phase. Proposers are likely to try small, low-risk trades that give them a clear gain while being plausibly appealing to at least one other player. Turtle, as the first proposer, may test acquiring blue (which is most valuable to them) using green or red. Gorilla, as second proposer, will test trading for red. Zebra, as third proposer, will experiment with shedding low-value colored chips for green or possibly trying to pick up a slight improvement in one color if a cheap opportunity seems plausible. Many proposals may be declined if misaligned with hidden values, but accepted trades will begin revealing directional preferences: who is eager to buy red, who is eager to buy blue, and who is willing to give up which colors for green.

After round 0, some information is implicitly revealed. Turtle’s willingness to trade away green for blue signals they value blue more than green, and zebra’s acceptance shows they value green at least as much as blue at the offered rate. Gorilla’s purchase of red for green, again from zebra, implies gorilla values red relatively more than green, while zebra is still comfortable exchanging colored chips for green at roughly 1:1. No one accepted zebra’s attempt to buy green with blue at that same symmetric rate, suggesting that both turtle and gorilla see blue as at least as valuable as green. Now holdings are skewed: turtle holds more blue and less green, gorilla holds more red and less green, and zebra holds more green and fewer red/blue.

In round 1, proposers can better target their offers. Turtle will likely keep trying to convert red or green into blue but must remember that zebra just sold some blue and may be becoming reluctant to give up more at 1:1. Gorilla can continue trying to acquire cheap red, perhaps from turtle this time, or may try to exploit zebra’s taste for green further. Zebra, now green-rich and color-poor, is likely to push in the opposite direction: trading green for either red or blue if the terms are slightly favorable, or trying to arbitrage small perceived mispricings. However, because everyone now has some evidence that blue and red are strongly valued by turtle and gorilla respectively, it becomes harder for zebra to obtain colored chips for green at very generous rates.

By the final round, trading patterns are clearer: turtle is a strong buyer of blue and reluctant to part with it; gorilla has established themselves as a consistent buyer of red and, after the last trade, also as a buyer of red for green; zebra has repeatedly sold colored chips for green and once bought red at roughly 1:1 with green. Everyone now has rough beliefs that turtle likes blue most, gorilla likes red most, and zebra is relatively more indifferent across colors but happy to accumulate green.

With no future rounds, there is no reason to hold back from any profitable trade. Turtle will attempt one last push to obtain blue, probably using red or remaining green, but counterparties now understand the relative scarcity and high value of blue and may demand better terms than simple 1:1 swaps, leading to potential rejections. Gorilla may still try to pick up cheap red either from zebra or turtle, but zebra has recently paid 1 green for 1 red, revealing that zebra values red at least at parity with green—so further 1:1 offers may fail. Zebra, aware that time is running out, may propose a final arbitrage or rebalancing move, such as trading some surplus green for whichever colored chip seems easiest to obtain. Because each player has a calculator and no continuation value, only clearly nonnegative deals from each agent’s perspective will be accepted. This yields a mix of one or two modest, nearly zero-surplus trades plus some final, overly aggressive proposals that get declined.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game is driven by asymmetric private values for red and blue chips, with green as a common-value baseline ($0.50 for everyone). All players start with 10 of each color. Privately, cat values red most (0.90) and blue moderately (0.60), iguana values red more than blue but both below cat’s blue (0.80 red, 0.30 blue), and zebra values blue very highly (1.00) and red weakly (0.40). Because players only see their own values, they must infer others’ valuations from the pattern of offers and acceptances over time. Core incentives: cat wants to accumulate red from zebra and iguana in exchange for blue or green; iguana wants to turn low-value blue into higher-value red, and may be willing to ship blue to zebra or cat; zebra will be eager to buy blue and willing to sell red, and has less reason to trade green unless the terms are very favorable. Each proposal is risky: accept decisions are simultaneous, and if more than one player accepts, the partner is chosen randomly, so proposers cannot target a specific counterpart. This pushes proposers to post deals that are at least weakly profitable for multiple players but not so generous that they give away all surplus. Early in the game, proposals likely probe who values which color more (e.g., offering blue for red or vice versa). Over the three rounds, as acceptance/rejection patterns accumulate, players gain noisy information about others’ valuations and may adjust toward more mutually beneficial trades: zebra trying to buy blue from iguana, cat trying to buy red from zebra or iguana, and iguana trying to sell blue while not getting fleeced on red. However, limited time (three rounds) and strategic uncertainty mean that some trades that would increase total surplus never occur, especially when a proposer fears making an offer that might be accepted by the “wrong” counterpart or that leaves too little surplus for themselves.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of round 0, no one has information about others’ private values beyond the public ranges. Players are likely to post exploratory offers that reflect their own priorities while testing market interest. Cat, who values red most, may begin by trying to cheaply acquire red or shed lower-valued colors for higher-valued ones. Iguana, with relatively low blue value, may try to convert blue or green into red or test whether others overvalue blue. Zebra, anticipating that someone might want to sell low-value blue, will probe how cheaply they can acquire blue while dumping some red. Rejections in this round mainly teach players which directions of trade are unattractive and whether at least one counterpart is on the same side of a color (e.g., both dislike blue or both like red), while any accepted trades reveal a profitable direction and hint at relative valuations.

After round 0, holdings and limited information have shifted. Zebra successfully traded with iguana, giving zebra extra blue and fewer red, and giving iguana extra red and fewer blue. This confirms for both that zebra is willing to pay red for blue and that iguana is happy to supply blue at a good red price. Cat, who sat out the accepted trade, now infers that at least one counterpart values blue more than red, which fits cat’s own desire to accumulate red. With this updated picture, players now cautiously refine their strategies: cat may post a more targeted attempt to buy red from the player most willing to part with it; iguana, having profited from selling blue, may either try to repeat that type of trade or diversify by offloading some green; zebra may continue to monetize low-value red for blue, but will also be aware that both others could bid for red, raising zebra’s price expectations. Uncertainty remains about exact valuations, but directions of comparative advantage are clearer, making mutually beneficial trades somewhat easier to identify if proposers do not set overly aggressive terms.

Entering the final round, the earlier accepted trades have reinforced a pattern: zebra repeatedly buys blue with red from iguana, who happily converts low-value blue into higher-value red. Cat has yet to complete any trade, suggesting that their previous offers were perceived as too stingy or misaligned with others’ valuations. Time pressure now matters: this is the last chance to improve payoffs, so players may be willing to relax margins slightly to secure at least one more profitable trade. Zebra, now with substantial blue and fewer red, still has an incentive to buy any remaining cheap blue, but only if the red price is reasonable; iguana, who has benefited from two trades, may still be open to modest additional gains but is less desperate. Cat, having missed out so far, may either post a more generous, simple offer to finally acquire red or may stick with conservative proposals that again get rejected. Overall, uncertainty is lower about relative rankings (everyone can see zebra tends to pay red for blue and iguana tends to accept), but exact valuations remain private, so there is a risk of over- or under-shooting in this last opportunity, yielding at most one more accepted trade.

parsed_target.game_explanation:
This 3-player bargaining game features privately known values for red and blue chips and a common known value for green chips. Each player starts with a symmetric 10/10/10 inventory but faces different tradeoffs: green is always $0.50; bear values both red and blue at $0.80 (no private preference between them), peacock values red at $0.80 and blue at $0.70 (slight red premium), and swan values blue at $0.80 and red at $0.50 (clear blue premium). Because players know only their own private values, they must infer others’ likely valuations from observed proposals and acceptances.

Individually rational trade requires that any accepted proposal make the responder strictly better off in dollar terms given their own values, which they can see via the surplus calculator. However, what is efficient for one pair might not be for another because of asymmetric values, and competitive pressure from a third player—combined with random selection when multiple accept—creates strategic uncertainty. Proposers prefer offers that (a) guarantee nonnegative surplus for at least one potential recipient, (b) do not leak too much value to the counterparty, and (c) might attract more than one potential responder without being so generous that they regret the random tie-break.

Key structural incentives: (1) Swan benefits greatly from converting red into blue and is relatively indifferent about green vs red (green $0.50, red $0.50) but likes both less than blue. (2) Peacock wants red slightly more than blue and is indifferent between red/blue and green trades that maintain or slightly improve expected value but is cautious about losing red too cheaply. (3) Bear is roughly happy to swap red for blue or vice versa but sees green as inferior; bear is a natural seller of green and neutral exchanger between red and blue. These differences create scope for multi-step arbitrage: e.g., bear offloads green for red or blue, then later trades red/blue to the color-specialized player (peacock for red, swan for blue).

Over only three rounds (nine total turns), players have limited time to learn about others’ types. Early offers will be relatively mild and information-seeking, giving at least one respondent a small gain while preserving margin for the proposer. As more trades occur, revealed acceptance patterns will suggest who especially values which colors, but with so few turns, players may never fully exploit the theoretically available gains. The random recipient rule means proposers tailor trades that are acceptable to both potential partners but slightly more attractive to the partner they hope is selected, accepting some probability-weighted loss of control. Overall, the likely game path includes modest, low-risk trades that move chips toward players with moderately higher valuations rather than aggressive or highly skewed bargains.

In the forecast below, the game path is one plausible, incentive-compatible trajectory where everyone remains locally rational given their private information and observed history, and no player knowingly takes a negative-surplus trade.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of round 0, all players hold 10 of each chip type and know only their own valuations. No one has yet learned anything about others’ preferences. Proposers will likely test the waters with conservative, small-volume offers that (a) give at least one potential responder a visible gain, (b) avoid revealing too aggressively which colors they themselves most value, and (c) try to identify who is more eager for red vs blue. Because green is common-value and known to be less valuable than red/blue for at least some types, offers involving green are natural early experiments: players can try trading green away for red or blue while leaving enough margin to attract acceptance from at least one counterpart.

By round 1, the only executed trade has been bear giving green for red from peacock, revealing that peacock is at least mildly willing to acquire green at par or slightly worse with respect to red. Bear has slightly upgraded their portfolio away from green and toward red; peacock has more green and less red, suggesting to observant players that peacock may value red somewhat but was still willing to part with a couple of red chips. No one has yet traded for blue, so relative blue preferences remain largely untested. Proposers now have weak signals: bear knows that at least one counterpart will sometimes buy green; peacock knows someone was willing to accept green-for-red terms; swan has only seen others’ behavior. The next set of offers can more directly probe red vs blue differentials, especially trying to move chips toward the specialized high-valuers while still being safe given the proposer’s own values.

Going into round 2, all players have seen evidence that swan is willing to trade in ways that increase their blue holdings, and that bear is comfortable exchanging green for red and blue. Peacock has just succeeded in swapping some blue for red with swan, signaling that swan is happy to offload red for blue even at terms that look somewhat unfavorable in simple chip counts. This pattern suggests, though not with certainty, that swan has a strong preference for blue over red; bear and peacock may update beliefs in that direction. With only one round left, players will try to execute remaining obviously beneficial rebalancing moves: swan may still seek more blue in exchange for red, bear may continue dumping residual green in favor of red/blue, and peacock may try one more red-favoring trade if an opportunity seems cheap. However, inventory constraints and prior trades limit how extreme these offers can be while still giving respondents positive surplus. Accepted trades in this final round likely fine-tune, rather than radically transform, the distribution of chips.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players start with symmetric chip quantities (10 of each color) but asymmetric private values for red and blue. Green is common-value at $0.50 for everyone, so trading green is a pure transfer of surplus between red/blue and a safe store of value. Camel and Monkey value blue chips very highly ($0.80 and $0.90) relative to red ($0.50 and $0.20), while Gorilla values red and blue symmetrically and relatively low ($0.30 each). From an omniscient perspective, the main surplus opportunities are transferring blue from Gorilla to Camel/Monkey and shifting some low-value red away from Monkey toward Camel or Gorilla, possibly via green as a balancing chip. However, each player knows only their own payoffs and can only infer others’ values from observed behavior, so proposals are guided by each sender’s own surplus calculator and simple heuristics: they tend to ask for what seems more valuable to them and offer what they personally value less, while trying to propose win–win trades that appear modest enough that at least one partner is likely to accept. Because multiple acceptances lead to a random partner, senders must design offers that are attractive to either or both potential recipients without tailoring to a specific one. Over just three rounds (nine total proposal turns), players face a tension between experimenting to learn about others’ values and locking in immediate gains; most behavior is therefore expected to be myopic and conservative, with small, clearly positive-sum trades rather than aggressive pooling or complex multi-step strategies. Early trades that reveal a counterpart’s willingness to exchange certain colors can alter later proposals as players infer relative valuations and compete subtly for the most valuable chips, especially blue.

rounds.round_explanation: In the first round, no one has yet observed any trades, so all players are essentially guessing about the others’ preferences beyond the public ranges. Proposers rely heavily on their surplus calculator and simple logic: offer some of what they value less and ask for what they value more, trying to design clearly beneficial deals that at least one counterpart might accept. Because acceptances are simultaneous and random among acceptors, senders try to keep offers symmetric enough that either potential recipient could plausibly find them profitable. The first accepted trades, if any, will start to reveal who seems to covet which colors, informing future proposals.

By the second round, Camel has successfully obtained blue from Gorilla in exchange for green, revealing that Gorilla is willing to give up blue for what appears to them as roughly similar or modestly better value, and that Camel strongly wants blue. Gorilla now holds extra green and fewer blue; Camel holds more blue and fewer green; Monkey’s holdings are unchanged but Monkey observes that Camel is willing to spend green on blue. Proposers start to update: offers are more likely to target Camel for blue trades and to use green as a balancing chip. However, substantial uncertainty remains about exact valuations, so proposals still tend to be small and approximately fair from the sender’s point of view.

Entering the final round, Camel has substantially increased blue holdings by trading away both green and red; Gorilla has become a net seller of blue in exchange for green and red, and Monkey has managed to acquire a small amount of additional blue from Gorilla at the end of round 1. Everyone now has a clearer picture: Gorilla appears relatively indifferent between red and blue and quite willing to exchange them for green or modest gains, Camel persistently chases blue even at the cost of red and green, and Monkey is selectively trying to pick up blue when it is cheap. Because this is the last round, there is no value in signaling; proposals are purely about extracting immediate surplus from any remaining opportunities given current imbalances in holdings. Players may be slightly more aggressive, but they still rely on small, evidently profitable trades due to limited knowledge of others’ exact values.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game features common-value green chips and privately valued red/blue chips, with each player starting at 10 of each color. Everyone knows all chip colors are worth something in [0.10, 1.00] and that green is exactly $0.50 to everyone, but they only know their own red/blue values. Because proposals are simultaneous-response and, if multiple accept, the partner is randomly chosen, proposers must think in terms of making an offer attractive enough that at least one counterpart gains, while understanding they cannot target a specific partner. Each player also has a surplus calculator revealing their own gain/loss on any posted trade, so no one will accept a trade that is strictly negative for them given their own values. The central strategic tension is between exploiting others’ possible lower valuations for some colors and avoiding overpaying for chips one privately values less. Given the symmetric starting endowments and only three rounds, players are likely to test small, plausibly beneficial exchanges early to infer others’ valuations. Over time, trade patterns can reveal rough relative preferences: a player eager to buy red may value red highly; one eager to buy blue likely values blue highly, etc. However, with only three rounds (nine total proposals), uncertainty remains significant, and some proposals will fail because they unintentionally make one or both potential recipients worse off. In this specific configuration, alligator (R=0.70, B=0.30) most values red, least values blue; otter (R=0.30, B=0.50) most values blue, least values red; tiger (R=0.60, B=0.20) also values red strongly and dislikes blue. Thus, there is natural scope for otter to supply red in exchange for blue or green, and for alligator and tiger to supply blue in exchange for red or green. Green, being common-value at $0.50, serves as a neutral medium: trades involving green are easier to accept because everyone computes the same per-chip value. Across the game, the likely pattern is a small number of accepted trades that transfer red from otter to alligator/tiger and blue from alligator/tiger to otter, plus possibly some green as a balancing chip, interspersed with some declined attempts that either mis-price the exchange or mistakenly aim to buy a color both potential partners also value highly.

rounds.round_explanation: In the first round, no one has observed any trade yet, so beliefs about others’ valuations are based only on the public ranges and the knowledge that red and blue values differ across players. All players start with 10 of each color. Proposers are likely to make conservative, low-risk offers that they think could be mutually beneficial under a wide range of counterpart valuations. A rational pattern is: (1) alligator tries to trade away blue (which they value least) in exchange for red or green; (2) otter tries to trade away red (which they value least) for blue or green; and (3) tiger mirrors alligator by trying to offload blue for red or green. Some of these offers will be declined when, for at least one potential recipient, the calculator shows a loss or zero gain; accepted trades will tend to be ones where the proposer’s low-value chip is someone else’s high-value chip, or where green is used at roughly fair value.

By the start of round 1, players have seen that trades exchanging low-valued colors for green or for others’ likely high-valued colors can be accepted. From round 0: (1) alligator successfully traded 2 BLUE for 1 GREEN, revealing at least one counterpart is willing to part with green for more than one blue; (2) otter traded 1 RED for 1 BLUE to tiger, suggesting that at least one counterpart is willing to give up blue at a 1:1 rate for red, hinting that red might be more valuable to them than to otter; (3) tiger traded 2 BLUE for 1 GREEN with otter, reinforcing that blue is relatively easy to offload. These outcomes hint to otter that others dislike blue and like red/green more, and to alligator and tiger that otter is willing to sell red and green under some conditions. Strategic behavior in this round shifts toward slightly more targeted exploitation of perceived comparative advantages: alligator and tiger try to buy red or green from otter using blue, and otter continues to shed red in exchange for blue or green where possible. Some offers become sharper in terms of surplus for the proposer, which raises the risk of rejection if they leave too little on the table for recipients.

Entering the final round, the trade history indicates that: blue is broadly disfavored (both alligator and tiger repeatedly tried to sell it), red is relatively more valued by at least alligator and tiger (they buy it when the rate is good), and green is stable at $0.50 for everyone and has been used as a balancing asset. Otter has been willing to sell both red and green when adequately compensated, while buying blue when it’s cheap, consistent with their lower valuation of red and higher valuation of blue. Alligator by now has strong evidence that trading away blue for red is feasible only when the rate is not too demanding for potential recipients; similarly, tiger has learned that extreme prices for red using blue are unlikely to clear. With only three proposal opportunities left (one each), players face a now-or-never choice: either lock in modest, safe gains via slightly favorable but still attractive offers, or attempt more aggressive trades that risk being declined. Because the partner is chosen at random among acceptors, proposers remain constrained to offers that would be at least weakly beneficial for any potential recipient they hope might accept.

parsed_target.game_explanation:
This three-player bargaining game features privately known values for red and blue chips, with green chips as a common-value currency at $0.50 each. Everyone begins with 10 of each chip, so initial payoffs are equal in dollar terms, but the potential gains from trade are large because each player values colors differently. Dolphin strongly prefers blue ($1.00) and dislikes red ($0.20); Frog strongly prefers red ($1.00) and dislikes blue ($0.30); Pig is symmetric at low value on red and blue ($0.40 each). All equally value green at $0.50. This induces clear cross-subsidies: Dolphin should want to buy blue and sell red; Frog should want to buy red and sell blue; Pig is best used as a side-partner in trades where they can get green (safe value) or a slightly favorable mix of red/blue.

However, players do not know others’ exact valuations, only a broad public range, so they must infer willingness to trade from observed behavior. Because responses to proposals are simultaneous and private, senders face uncertainty about which counterpart (if any) will accept. When multiple accept, the partner is randomly chosen, so proposals must remain profitable regardless of which acceptor they end up with. Each player uses an on-screen surplus calculator that reveals their own net gain or loss from any proposed trade, ensuring they will not rationally accept trades with negative private surplus. As a result, accepted trades will typically show a clearly positive surplus for the recipient and at least weakly positive surplus for the proposer.

Over three rounds (nine total proposal turns), we should expect early proposals to be relatively cautious and exploratory, using green as a neutral transfer and moderate markups to test others’ willingness. As players see which trades are accepted, they update their beliefs about others’ preferences (e.g., inferring that someone who eagerly buys red probably values red highly). The binding constraints are: (1) proposers cannot offer more than they hold; (2) each proposal is a simple two-color swap; and (3) only one trade can execute per turn. Given symmetry and time-limited interaction, players are likely to converge on simple, intuitive trades: Dolphin trades away red for blue or green; Frog trades away blue for red; Pig opportunistically trades when the surplus gap is clearly in its favor, often taking green for red or blue or accepting modestly beneficial red–blue swaps. The following forecast reconstructs one plausible path of play consistent with these incentives and with individually rational accept/decline decisions at each step.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start, all players hold 10 of each color and have no direct evidence about the others’ values. Proposals in this round are likely to be simple and use green as a safe benchmark. Dolphin is strongly blue-loving and red-averse, so will tentatively try to dump red for either blue or green at a mild markup. Frog is strongly red-loving and blue-averse, so will likely try to trade away blue for red using green as a balancing chip if needed. Pig, whose red and blue are moderate and equal, can profit by taking clearly favorable offers on either side, especially when green is on the table. The uncertainty over partner selection when multiple accept will encourage all senders to offer trades that would be acceptable with either of the other two players.

By the second round, trade history has revealed partial information. Dolphin’s initial red-for-blue trade signaled a strong preference for blue over red. Frog’s willingness to trade away blue for red suggested the opposite orientation. Pig revealed a willingness to convert red into green, hinting that red is not especially valuable to them compared with the safe green benchmark. Holdings are now uneven, so proposers must respect stock constraints and adjust proposals based on who still has surplus of each color. Players will start targeting more refined trades: Dolphin continues trying to shed remaining low-value red in exchange for high-value blue or stable green, while Frog and Pig respond only when the calculator shows a solid gain.

In the final round, players face a deadline and have a clearer picture of relative valuations inferred from prior trades. Dolphin appears consistently eager to upgrade red into blue or green; Frog has consistently moved toward more red and green while offloading blue; Pig has exploited small, nearly symmetric improvements and safe green conversions. With only one proposal turn left for each player, they are likely to look for remaining easy improvements and avoid risky, marginal trades. Uncertainty about partner choice in the event of multiple acceptances still matters, so each proposal must be universally safe across potential counterparts. The equilibrium tendency is toward modest, clearly beneficial swaps rather than aggressive attempts to extract large surpluses that might be rejected.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game features private values for red and blue chips and a common value for green chips. Everyone starts with 10 of each color, and each player only knows their own red/blue values. From an analyst view, largest surplus trades convert chips away from a player who values that color relatively little toward a player who values it more, while giving back chips in a color where the direction is reversed. Here, owl strongly values red (0.70) and weakly values blue (0.30), iguana strongly values blue (0.80) and weakly values red (0.20), and squid is intermediate on blue (0.50) and very low on red (0.10). Green is neutral at 0.50 for everyone, so early trades often use green as a risk-free currency when players are unsure about others’ values for red/blue. Because acceptance decisions are private and simultaneous, senders must offer trades that are at least weakly attractive to at least one other player, but they are also uncertain which counterpart (if any) will bite. Over three rounds with a fixed proposal order, proposers can condition on observed acceptances to infer who likes which color, but they cannot learn others’ exact values. We should expect moderately generous, simple offers early (often involving green for one non-common chip) that probe the market. As players observe who accepts what, offers can become more targeted: iguana tries to accumulate blue, owl tries to accumulate red, and squid opportunistically takes positive-margin deals in either red or blue while using green as payment when helpful. However, due to incomplete information and random recipient selection when multiple accept, not all potential surplus will be realized and some proposals will be declined when they leave the responder with a clear loss.

rounds.round_explanation: In the first round, no one has seen any behavior yet, so players mainly probe. Risk-averse, privately-informed agents tend to use green chips—whose value they know is common—as payment or as a safe reference. Iguana, who highly values blue and not red, will tentatively try to use low-value red to gain green, hoping that at least one counterpart values green more than red. Owl, who values red highly and blue poorly, will experiment with using blue or green to acquire red. Squid, with very low red value and middling blue, will test whether others are willing to give up blue or green in exchange for red. This round establishes basic directional preferences via acceptances and rejections rather than perfectly efficient trades.

By round 1, each player has a small amount of behavioral information. Iguana has revealed a willingness to accept blue-for-red trades that look favorable given iguana’s high blue and low red valuation. Owl has shown an eagerness to trade away blue for red. Squid has used low-value red to get blue. This pattern indicates to others, at least qualitatively, that owl likes red more than blue, iguana likes blue more than red, and squid is happy to unload red for blue. Now proposers can offer more targeted, somewhat more aggressive deals that still leave at least one counterpart with a positive surplus. Green remains a flexible, safe currency to make deals more palatable when the proposer worries a pure red–blue trade might be borderline for responders.

In the final round, players are more confident in each other’s directional preferences but still face value uncertainty, and there is no future to discipline behavior, so they push harder for advantageous terms. Iguana, now clearly oriented toward blue and with a stock of red and some green, will try to convert remaining red into blue on favorable terms. Owl, having progressively acquired red and traded away some blue and green, will still accept any strongly profitable red-acquiring deal but will be cautious about giving up too much surplus this late. Squid will look for any last opportunities to turn low-value red into blue or green, or to make small positive-margin trades involving green. Because this is the last chance to trade, responders are stricter: many marginal deals that might have been accepted earlier for strategic or exploratory reasons are now declined unless they offer a clear immediate gain.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game features heterogeneous private values for red and blue chips and a common value for green chips. Gorilla-purple-8501 values blue the least and red moderately, while llama-pink-7039 and whale-orange-533 both value red and blue highly and symmetrically. All three value green at $0.50, making green a natural numeraire and low-friction trading medium. Because players only know their own private values, early moves are partly exploratory: proposers will tentatively test whether others value certain colors more by posting trades that are strictly beneficial for themselves but might still leave some surplus for a counterparty with different valuations. Acceptance decisions, however, are fully rational at the individual level because each responder sees their own net gain or loss in real time. The main efficiency and bargaining tension arises from asymmetric information about others’ values and the random selection of the partner when multiple players accept. Gorilla is naturally inclined to dump low-value blue chips and possibly acquire green (safe value) or some red, while llama and whale want to accumulate both red and blue if the price in green or the other color is favorable. But because llama and whale share identical preferences, they become direct competitors for profitable offers from gorilla and may sometimes bid against each other via simultaneous acceptance. Over three rounds, strategic considerations include (1) extracting informational signals from who accepts what, (2) anticipating that future proposers might offer better terms, and (3) accounting for the risk of being undercut by the competitor with similar preferences. The forecasted play below emphasizes individually rational, mildly exploratory, and surplus-seeking behavior consistent with those incentives and uncertainties.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start, everyone has symmetric chip endowments but different private values. Gorilla, with low blue value, has a strong incentive to offer blue in exchange for green or red to test counterpart valuations. Llama and whale, who both strongly value red and blue, will look for cheap ways to convert green into higher-value colors but will avoid trades that reduce their total dollar payoff. Round 0 proposals are likely to be simple one-color-for-one-color trades that minimize strategic complexity while probing the market. Because no history yet exists, responders must infer others’ values solely from the generosity or stinginess of early offers.

After a fully declined first round, players learn that very aggressive, one-sided trades are unlikely to be accepted: others plainly rejected clearly unprofitable offers. Gorilla now has weak evidence that blue might be more valuable to at least one counterpart but not at an extreme green-for-blue rate. Llama and whale see that neither was willing to overpay in green for red or blue. In round 1, proposers may shade their offers closer to break-even for the responder, introducing small but positive surplus for the counterparty while still improving their own position. Because no trades have executed yet, holdings are still symmetric, so bargaining dynamics focus on price discovery rather than inventory imbalances.

With two full rounds of rejection, all players have inferred that others are quite strict about not accepting negative-surplus trades. Gorilla, in particular, has learned that neither llama nor whale will surrender green for blue at a loss, and llama and whale have seen that gorilla will not trade away higher-value red or green too cheaply. The final round creates a deadline effect: it is the last chance to realize any surplus. This can induce more generous offers that give a small positive gain to the counterparty while maintaining a gain for the proposer. A natural candidate is for gorilla to sell blue for green at a rate that benefits both sides, and for llama or whale to accept if the calculator shows a positive net change. Once one mutually beneficial trade reveals clearer relative values, subsequent proposers are more willing to post similar win–win deals. Competition between llama and whale for favorable terms from gorilla increases the likelihood that at least one of them accepts a modestly generous offer in this final round.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players begin with symmetric chip counts (10 RED, 10 BLUE, 10 GREEN) but asymmetric private values for RED and BLUE, while GREEN is a common-value chip at $0.50 for everyone. Tiger highly values both RED ($0.80) and BLUE ($1.00); Duck values RED and GREEN equally ($0.50) but BLUE weakly ($0.20); Lion is intermediate on BLUE ($0.30) and slightly weaker on RED ($0.40) and GREEN ($0.50). From an analyst perspective, Tiger is the strongest demander of both colored chips and will gladly trade away GREEN (low marginal relative to RED/BLUE for them) to acquire RED or BLUE. Duck wants to turn low-value BLUE into higher-value RED or GREEN, and is comparatively indifferent between RED and GREEN. Lion sits in the middle and can sometimes be a bridge in deals if they can buy low from Duck and sell high to Tiger, but they do not see the others’ values and will mostly act myopically on visible surplus. Uncertainty arises because each player only knows their own values: they know others may value colors differently, but not how much. Therefore they must experiment with price ratios (e.g., how many BLUE they can get for one GREEN) and infer others’ values from acceptance behavior. Because responders see exact personal surplus from a proposal, nobody rationally accepts a strictly negative-surplus trade; accepted trades should produce weakly positive surplus for the chosen responder. The proposer also sees their own surplus calculator and should avoid negative-surplus offers. However, they cannot choose which acceptor becomes the partner when multiple responders accept, so they normally design offers that would be acceptable (or at least non-negative) against either responder, limiting very extreme price discrimination. Over three rounds (nine total turns), we should see early, exploratory trades offering apparently generous terms to elicit acceptance, followed by more targeted exploitation once someone realizes that, for example, Tiger is an eager buyer of BLUE or RED. Given Tiger’s especially strong value for BLUE, we expect Tiger to be the main net buyer of BLUE over the game (often paying GREEN or, more rarely, RED), and Duck to be the main net seller of BLUE. Lion is likely to make modest, safe trades that give both sides clear surplus, and may be slightly more cautious given their mid-range valuations and the risk of giving up too much surplus if they misjudge others’ preferences.

rounds.round_explanation: In the first round, no trades have yet occurred, so beliefs about others’ values are very diffuse. Each player knows that colors can be valued differently and that GREEN is common-value, but they do not know exactly how much the others like RED versus BLUE. Proposers are likely to make simple, clearly surplus-positive offers from their own point of view, possibly skewed toward using GREEN as a payment currency because its common value is known to be moderate for everyone. Trades that reveal strong demand (such as someone quickly accepting a BLUE-for-GREEN trade) will shape subsequent turns, but in this opening round each proposal is partly exploratory: offering terms that are a bit generous to entice at least one acceptance and learn who is on which side of the market.

After round 0, some information has been revealed. Duck has signaled a willingness to trade away a lot of BLUE for a modest amount of RED, suggesting to others that Duck does not value BLUE highly relative to RED. Tiger’s quick acceptance of that trade suggests Tiger values RED more than BLUE (or at least significantly values RED). The second accepted trade, where Tiger bought BLUE for GREEN from Lion at what turned out to be a very favorable price for Lion, shows that Tiger is also a strong buyer of BLUE and is willing to pay GREEN. Lion now infers that Tiger has relatively high value for BLUE and perhaps for RED as well. Duck has not yet interacted directly with GREEN, and Lion has learned that trading away GREEN can be profitable if the price in BLUE is high enough. In round 1, we should see more targeted trades: Duck likely tries to sell more BLUE or pick up GREEN if the price seems good; Lion may attempt further trades with Tiger but be more cautious on the BLUE-for-GREEN margin; Tiger continues to demand RED and BLUE but tries to avoid overpaying in GREEN now that others might suspect their high valuations.

Going into the final round, everyone has richer information. Duck has repeatedly sold BLUE and once bought GREEN; this strongly signals to others that BLUE is Duck’s least valued color and that Duck is comfortable holding more RED and some GREEN. Lion has made one profitable sale of GREEN for BLUE to Tiger early on and one profitable purchase of BLUE from Duck, and has seen Tiger accept trades that pay with GREEN for both BLUE and RED. This pattern confirms for Lion that Tiger is systematically high-valuing both RED and BLUE and is willing to use GREEN as a payment chip. Tiger, from their side, has seen Duck gladly sell BLUE cheaply and accept GREEN for RED, confirming that Duck’s value ranking likely puts GREEN and RED above BLUE. In this last round, proposers balance two motives: squeezing a bit more surplus out of already-revealed comparative advantages (especially Tiger buying BLUE/RED and Duck selling BLUE) and avoiding offers that transfer too much surplus in the wrong direction. Because it is the final chance to trade, players no longer need to worry about signaling for future rounds; they instead focus on any remaining obviously positive-surplus opportunities, even if they give good deals to opponents, as long as their own calculator stays strongly positive.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players start symmetric in chip counts (10 of each color) but differ in how much they privately value red and blue chips; green chips are common-value at $0.50 for everyone. Bear and Goose both value red highly ($1.00) and blue weakly ($0.30 and $0.20), while Ram values blue highly ($1.00) and red less ($0.80). This creates a strong underlying efficiency motive to move blue chips toward Ram and red chips toward Bear and Goose. However, no one knows others’ exact values—only that they lie in a general public range—so bargaining mixes learning and surplus extraction. Proposers see their own surplus calculator and will avoid trades that look negative to them; responders see their own surplus and will almost never accept strictly negative trades. With two potential counterparties for every proposal and random recipient selection when multiple players accept, proposers must design offers that are good enough for at least one responder but not so generous that they give away surplus unnecessarily or risk benefitting the “wrong” counterparty. Bear and Goose both suspect that someone may value blue more than they do; Ram suspects that others may value red more than he does.
