parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game is driven by asymmetric private values over red and blue chips and a common value for green chips. Each player starts with 10 chips of each color. Green chips are worth $0.50 to everyone, so they are a safe, transparent store of value; nobody expects to gain by trading green directly for green, but they might use green as a medium to buy underpriced colors or to sell overpriced ones. The key tension is that each player only sees their own values: they know green is exactly $0.50 for everyone (because the range is only $0.50–$0.50), but for red and blue they only know the common range ($0.10–$1.00), not others’ exact values. As analysts, we know: alligator values BLUE highly ($0.60) and RED very low ($0.10); otter values BLUE at $1.00 and RED at $0.40; tiger values BLUE at $0.90 and RED at $0.70. So blue is always more valuable than red for everyone, but the spreads differ. Green is dominated by blue for all, and by red for otter and tiger, but green strictly dominates red for alligator. This creates scope for mutually beneficial trades: (1) blue should tend to flow toward otter and tiger, especially toward otter who has the highest blue value; (2) red should tend to flow away from alligator toward tiger (who has the highest red value) and somewhat toward otter; (3) green is most valuable for alligator relative to their low red value, so alligator is the most willing to pay green to shed red or to gain blue. However, because proposals must specify only one color to buy and one different color to sell, and because only one partner can be randomly selected when multiple accept, proposers have to balance surplus extraction with the risk of being undercut by an unexpectedly eager partner. The surplus calculator makes sure that any accepted trade is weakly beneficial in private value terms at the moment of acceptance. Over three rounds (nine proposal turns), we expect early moves to be exploratory and conservative, especially in Round 0 as players test demand and avoid visible losses. As trading history accumulates, players infer that others likely value blue highly and may adjust offers accordingly, pushing toward more balanced splits of surplus. Random tie-breaking when multiple accept may lead to some trades going to slightly less efficient partners, but all accepted trades will still be privately profitable for both sides. In the forecast below, we construct a plausible path where players discover profitable red-for-blue and red-for-green exchanges, with alligator progressively upgrading away from red into blue and green, otter accumulating blue while spending red and some green, and tiger opportunistically grabbing red when the terms are favorable, especially once others reveal their eagerness for blue.

rounds.round_explanation: In Round 0, everyone has symmetric chip holdings (10 of each color), but asymmetric private values. Players know green is exactly $0.50 for everyone, so they treat it as safe money. They suspect blue may be relatively valuable to others, but don’t know the exact rankings of red versus blue for others. Conservative first offers are likely: small quantities, modest markups over the fair $0.50 green benchmark, and attempts to trade away personally weak colors. Alligator wants to dump low-value red and gain blue or green; otter wants blue most and is willing to give up red and some green; tiger wants both blue and red, with a slight bias toward blue. No one yet has information from past trades, so accepted deals will mainly be low-risk, clearly positive exchanges where both sides see a gain according to the calculator. Declined offers will often reflect either too aggressive pricing or uncertainty about counterpart valuations.

By Round 1, two profitable trades have occurred: alligator and otter each bought blue from tiger in exchange for red. This publicly reveals that tiger is willing to give up blue for red at relatively favorable blue-for-red ratios, and that both alligator and otter are active blue demanders. Alligator has already upgraded a block of very low-value red into high-value blue, and otter has done similarly, though with a smaller red discount. Tiger, having accumulated red, now knows that others value blue enough to part with red, and that both counterparties declined tiger’s somewhat unattractive red-for-green attempt. This suggests green is not underpriced by others and red-for-green at a low red price is unappealing. In this round, we expect slightly bolder offers: alligator may try to use green to buy more blue; otter may offer green or red to continue buying blue but will start to push prices lower, and tiger will probe for better compensation for parting with blue, or seek to offload some of his growing red pile for green at more favorable ratios. Declines will occur when a proposal leaves the responder with only tiny gains or when better alternatives are anticipated.

Entering Round 2, substantial reallocation has occurred. Alligator has transformed a large share of low-value red into blue and green, becoming quite blue-rich and red-poor. Otter has heavily invested in blue as well, spending both red and green, and now holds a strong blue position relative to others. Tiger, after initially selling blue for red, has partly reversed course by selling red and buying green and some blue, ending up with a mixed portfolio that is less extreme than after Round 0. Publicly observed trades now clarify that all three players value blue highly and are wary of giving it up cheaply; meanwhile, red is recognized as somewhat weaker, especially by alligator. Green serves as a balancing asset: all three have been willing to trade green when the implied price per chip leaves them with a clear surplus. With only one round left, each proposer is looking for remaining win–win opportunities without exposing themselves to a visible loss. Margins on blue-for-red or blue-for-green trades become thinner, and some attractive-looking offers from the proposer’s perspective get declined because responders anticipate that better deals are unlikely and refuse trades with only tiny gains. The final accepted deals are those with straightforward, solid positive surplus for both sides, while very aggressive price attempts get rejected.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players start with symmetric chip counts but asymmetric money values for red and blue. Green is common-value ($0.50 for everyone), so green trades mainly serve to express or infer red/blue values or to rebalance portfolios, not to exploit value differences. Frog has a very strong preference for red ($0.90) and also likes blue ($0.50); pig moderately values red and blue ($0.40 and $0.30); dolphin values both red and blue very weakly ($0.10 each). Socially efficient trades move red from dolphin to frog/pig and blue from dolphin to frog/pig, with dolphin receiving green in return, because dolphin gives up low-value chips and receives high-value green. However, during play each player only sees their own values and must infer the others’ preferences from which proposals they make and which trades they accept. The surplus calculator means no one will knowingly accept a negative-value trade; all accepted trades must be individually rational in dollar terms. Proposers face uncertainty over which opponent—if any—will accept, and when both opponents accept, the random recipient rule makes it impossible to target a specific partner. This pushes them toward deals that are at least weakly attractive to both, or to offers that are clearly favorable to at least one but not obviously exploitable. Early in the game, players experiment with small, safe trades using green as a medium of exchange. As history accumulates, they adjust offers: dolphin learns that giving up red and blue for green is profitable and starts selling more of those; frog experiments with buying red and sometimes blue, but must offer enough green or blue to entice someone; pig plays more opportunistically, accepting any clearly positive mispricing and occasionally proposing moderate spreads. Time pressure (three rounds with three proposals each) and the symmetric proposer schedule encourage relatively simple, one-color-for-one-color exchanges where at least one non-sender sees an immediate gain on the calculator.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start, everyone has 10 of each chip and no information about others’ values beyond the public ranges. Rational players will test the waters with small, low-risk trades that look at least weakly favorable given plausible opponent valuations. Dolphin, as a low-value holder of red and blue, is naturally inclined to try selling those for green, though they do not yet know how much others might pay. Frog, who privately loves red, will probe whether others are willing to trade red away cheaply, but may start cautiously, using green or blue as payment. Pig will likely act as a middle-of-the-road trader, looking for clearly positive margins while watching how the others respond. In this first round, offers are exploratory, with modest quantities and prices chosen to elicit informative accept/decline patterns rather than to extract maximum surplus.

After round 0, some structure is revealed. Dolphin has shown willingness to trade away red for green and to buy blue with green; pig has accepted those deals, signaling that pig values green at least as much as what they gave up. Frog has managed to buy red from pig using green, reinforcing frog’s strong private preference for red. Everyone now knows that both opponents are willing to move red and blue under some prices. Dolphin has slightly more blue and fewer green/red; frog has more red and fewer green; pig has more green and fewer red/blue. In this round, players refine their strategies: dolphin increasingly positions as a seller of low-value red/blue in exchange for green; frog tries to accumulate more red but must pay better terms now that pig has learned red is in demand; pig arbitrages between frog’s strong red demand and dolphin’s weak red value, plus any remaining blue mispricing.

By the final round, patterns are clear: dolphin has consistently grabbed green when it’s cheap relative to their low red/blue values; frog has focused on acquiring red; pig has repeatedly taken positive spreads by buying green with red/blue and occasionally selling red to frog. Everyone has some idea that dolphin is tolerant of giving up red/blue, that frog eagerly buys red, and that pig values green enough to use it as a pivot asset. With only three proposals left, players try to lock in any remaining obvious gains. Dolphin is willing to sacrifice additional low-value colored chips if a favorable green trade appears. Frog is still happy to buy red even on modestly favorable terms, especially from dolphin, whose opportunity cost in red is low. Pig looks for one last arbitrage, but must avoid overpaying now that both others have had time to update. The final trades therefore tend to be slightly more aggressive, but still must show as strictly positive on each accepter’s surplus calculator.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game features a common-value green chip ($0.50 for everyone) and privately valued red and blue chips. All players strictly prefer red and blue to green at the margin: iguana (R=$0.30, B=$0.20) values green more than either red or blue; owl (R=$1.00, B=$0.60) and squid (R=$0.90, B=$0.50) value both red and blue more than green. This creates a strong directional incentive: iguana wants to accumulate green by giving up red/blue, while owl and squid want to convert green (and possibly some blue) into more red and blue chips. However, players do not know others’ exact values—only that red/blue are somewhere between $0.10 and $1.00 for others, and green is common-value. That uncertainty leads them to test rates of trade (e.g., how many greens for one red) that still show a nonnegative surplus in their own calculator, but they will generally start with favorable ratios and soften if they see no one accepts. A key strategic tension is competition between owl and squid: they have similar preferences and are competing to trade with iguana, but if both accept a favorable offer from iguana, a random recipient is chosen, so each risks not being picked. This can make them willing to accept slightly less favorable rates just to secure some trade. Over three rounds (nine total proposal turns), rational, calculator-using players will rarely accept negative-surplus trades; we therefore expect most executed trades to strictly improve both sides’ payoffs, typically with iguana selling red or blue for green at a rate that looks generous enough for at least one of owl or squid to accept. Later rounds may show adjusted proposals based on prior acceptance patterns and updated holdings, but the overall direction—green flowing to owl/squid in exchange for red/blue flowing to iguana—should persist.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of the game, everyone has symmetric endowments (10 of each color) but asymmetric values. None yet knows others’ valuations, only that red/blue vary by person. Early proposals are likely exploratory, with senders testing trade ratios that are slightly favorable to themselves but still obviously profitable for at least one counterpart. Iguana, who values green most, will likely try to trade away red or blue to get green. Owl and squid, who both value red/blue more than green, will look for chances to give up green to gain red/blue. Because it is the first round, all players are cautious yet optimistic; they will not accept a negative-surplus deal but will accept clearly positive ones, especially when the gain is obvious even under pessimistic assumptions about others’ values.

Going into round 1, there has been exactly one trade: owl gave squid 1 red in exchange for 3 green. Publicly observable holdings reveal that squid was willing to give up multiple green chips for a single red, which suggests to others that squid values red strongly relative to green. Iguana’s initial attempt to buy green with red was rejected at a 2G:1R rate, teaching iguana that owl and squid both value red at least moderately. Players now adjust: iguana may offer more generous rates when buying green; owl, having successfully traded with squid, might probe whether similar or better deals can be struck with iguana; squid, who just paid a high price in green for red, may become more selective about giving up green and instead look for opportunities to trade blue or surplus red. With a finite horizon of three rounds, players may still experiment but they also recognize that time is limited and will be more willing to accept modest but positive gains.

By the final round, several informative trades have occurred. Iguana has successfully converted some red and blue into green, revealing to others that iguana is relatively willing to give up red/blue for green, which is consistent with iguana’s higher value on green. Owl’s and squid’s behaviors revealed that red and blue are both quite valuable to them, especially owl’s strong willingness to receive red for green and squid’s initial eagerness to buy red with green. With only three proposal turns remaining, players face an endgame effect: there is no future bargaining, so they have no incentive to hold out for better future deals. As long as a trade offers even a small positive surplus, they are more likely to accept. This encourages somewhat more generous offers from senders that still remain strictly profitable on their calculators. We therefore expect at least one or two additional mutually beneficial trades, often involving iguana offloading remaining low-value red/blue in exchange for green, and owl/squid fine-tuning their portfolios of red and blue using up some of their remaining green.

Gorilla is likely to test trading away blue for red or green, Llama to trade away red or green for blue, and Whale to explore offloading some blue. Whale’s willingness to accept Gorilla’s blue-for-red offer but not engage Llama’s later trade signals that Whale is happy to swap some red for blue when the terms are favorable, while still treating blue as somewhat weak. Gorilla will push one last time to convert blue into red; Llama looks for a final blue-boosting opportunity; Whale attempts to unload a block of blue for either red or green, but the others now recognize blue’s likely low value for Whale and resist unfavorable rates. Green chips are common-value at $0.50, so everyone is indifferent about moving green except as a way to induce trade in higher-margin chips. Red chips are highly valued by duck ($1.00), lion ($0.90), and tiger ($1.00). Blue chips are valuable for duck ($1.00), moderate for lion ($0.50), and low for tiger ($0.30). Because each player only knows their own values, they must infer others’ valuations from observed offers and acceptances. Duck strongly values both red and blue, so duck is happy to buy red or blue using green as a nearly neutral currency, but will try to avoid giving away blue for green. Lion sees red almost as good as duck and tiger do, but blue is only worth half as much as to duck, suggesting lion may be willing to sell blue for the right price in red or green. Tiger values red like duck but blue much less, so tiger is the most natural blue seller and red buyer, likely wanting trades where they give up blue for red or even for green if the ratio is favorable. Uncertainty comes from not knowing others’ exact private valuations: each agent can see that all chip types have plausible ranges, but not that green is exactly 0.50 for all and that others’ blue values differ. Due to the random tie-breaking when multiple players accept, proposers must anticipate that especially attractive offers could be accepted by more than one counterpart, making targeting a specific partner risky. Over three rounds with fixed proposer order, early offers are likely exploratory and somewhat conservative, using small trades to test how eager others are to buy or sell particular colors. As players observe who accepts which trades, they can update beliefs and refine offers in later rounds. Efficient surplus-generating trades typically involve tiger selling blue to duck or lion, and lion potentially trading some red for blue or green if misaligned beliefs make that look favorable. But because they see exact personal surplus numbers at decision time, all acceptances must be personally profitable ex post, even if not fully efficient from the group perspective.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of the game, all players hold 10 of each chip and only know their own valuations. They have no direct evidence yet about others’ preferences, so first-round offers are likely to be small, low-risk probes. Duck, with high values on both red and blue, will likely experiment with a trade that uses green as payment to acquire more red or blue, expecting others to be somewhat indifferent about green. Lion may tentatively offer blue or green to learn how much others value these chips, while protecting red which they nearly value at duck and tiger’s level. Tiger, knowing they like red but not blue, is inclined to propose trades where they sell blue, possibly for green or red, to test if others see blue as more valuable. Everyone is watching initial accept/decline patterns to infer where the profitable directions of trade lie.

After round 0, the observed trades reveal early information. Duck declined to give up highly valued blue for green, signaling duck likely values blue strongly. Lion’s accepted trade exchanging green for blue with duck suggests lion’s value for blue is at least not too low relative to green, and that duck is happy gaining blue while losing green. Tiger’s proposal selling blue for green and lion’s acceptance show that lion is also willing to acquire blue from tiger at roughly even terms with green, while tiger is happy to offload low-value blue for green that is worth more to them. These patterns hint that tiger values blue less than others. Going into round 1, duck remains rich in blue and low in green compared to the start, lion has shifted toward more blue and fewer green, and tiger has fewer blue and more green. Strategically, further opportunities lie in trades that move blue away from tiger and toward duck or lion, and possibly trades where red is used to compensate for these imbalances once someone reveals willingness to trade red.

By the final round, the history reveals clearer patterns: duck has consistently tried to acquire valuable red and blue while paying in green, suggesting both colored chips are high value to duck; lion has been willing to trade green and sometimes contemplate red-for-blue trades, indicating moderate preferences for blue and strong but not absolute attachment to red; tiger repeatedly sells blue, revealing blue is their weakest chip. Holdings are now skewed: duck has accumulated both red and blue at the cost of green, lion holds substantial blue and somewhat reduced red and green, and tiger has shifted toward green and red while reducing blue. With only one proposal left per player, each has an incentive to make a clearly positive, individually safe trade rather than an aggressive, uncertain one. Duck may still try to use remaining green to buy more red or blue; lion may attempt a small rebalancing trade if a partner seems willing; tiger will still want to offload any remaining blue for better-valued chips if possible. However, the window for experimentation is closed, so players focus on straightforward net-gain offers that at least one counterpart is likely to accept based on revealed behavior.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players start with 10 chips of each color and know that green is common-value at $0.50 for everyone, but each only knows their own red/blue values. Bear strongly prefers blue ($1.00) and dislikes red ($0.20). Goose values both red and blue at $0.80. Ram values blue at $0.80 and red at $0.50. Because green is fixed at $0.50 for everyone, trades that move green for red or blue are usually zero-sum or negative-sum ex ante; thus the richest surplus comes from swapping red and blue across players with different valuations. Bear in particular wants to trade away red for blue, while goose and ram are more balanced and willing to accept advantageous red↔blue trades. The main uncertainty is about others’ private red/blue values: players infer from who accepts what. Bear’s early offers are likely exploratory, trying to move cheap red for green or blue. Goose and ram compare any offered trade against their own calculator, only accepting strict positive gains. Over three short rounds, players face pressure to capture obvious arbitrage (e.g., trades that help both sides by shifting blue toward bear or red toward goose) while avoiding over-generous offers or giveaways to a third party when both responders might accept. Random assignment of the recipient when multiple acceptors exist makes proposers cautious about offering deals that are strongly favorable to both possible recipients, because the proposer cannot target the partner. Overall, we expect a handful of simple, clearly positive-sum trades to be executed, with some marginal or exploratory offers being declined once responders see that the surplus for them is zero or negative.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of the game all players hold 10 of each chip. No one yet has information about others’ private red/blue values beyond the common ranges. Bear moves first and is most eager to turn low-value red into higher-value colors, but must keep offers that still look acceptable to at least one responder. Goose and ram are symmetric in blue, but goose values red more, so goose is somewhat more interested in accumulating red if the price is right. In this opening round, we expect mostly small, exploratory proposals aimed at testing willingness to trade and establishing who is more eager for red or blue.

After round 0, bear has successfully converted some red into blue twice, revealing to observers that bear seems happy to part with red and eager to gain blue. Goose has picked up extra red in the first trade but saw no gain in trading red for green in their own proposal. Ram has traded one red for one blue with bear, learning that bear is still willing to give up red cheaply. Everyone now infers that red is relatively low value for bear, somewhat high for goose, and moderate for ram. Second-round proposals are likely to refine this pattern: bear will continue seeking blue for red, goose may try to monetize their now larger red pile or pick up blue cheaply, and ram will look for modest improvements that do not give up too much red without adequate blue compensation.

Heading into the final round, trade history has clarified preferences: bear consistently accepts swapping red for blue at or near 1:1, signaling a strong preference for blue over red. Goose has at times sold blue for red, suggesting red is not much worse than blue for them, and has declined red-for-green trades that would not increase their value. Ram has twice accepted 1:1 red-for-blue swaps as the blue giver, confirming they still view red as somewhat less valuable than blue but are willing to do minor trades that look roughly value-neutral. With limited turns left, players attempt small, clearly beneficial moves rather than large speculative ones, ensuring no responder with negative surplus accepts. Because multiple-acceptor risk remains, proposers avoid offers that would be highly attractive to both potential recipients unless they are comfortable with either outcome.

parsed_target.game_explanation:
This three-player bargaining game revolves around trading red and blue chips, which have different private values to each player, while green chips are common-value and therefore neutral in terms of purely informational asymmetry. Everyone starts with 10 of each chip, knows only their own values, and can see trade history and quantities but not others’ valuations. The key tension is that some players strongly want blue chips and care relatively little about red, while others care more about red or are nearly indifferent; green is worth $0.50 to everyone and so is primarily used as a stable, low-risk medium of exchange.

Given the valuations: lion-blue-472 values blue at $0.60 and red/green at $0.50 each; octopus-green-4361 values blue very little ($0.10), red at $0.20, and green at $0.50; ram-purple-2824 values blue highly ($1.00), green at $0.50, and red at $0.30. Thus, octopus is blue-poor in value but rich in green; ram is blue-hungry and not fond of red; lion is relatively balanced and mildly prefers blue to red/green. The core positive-sum opportunities are: ram buying blue from octopus (who doesn’t value blue much) using red or green as payment; ram buying blue from lion if the price is favorable; lion buying green from octopus in exchange for blue if octopus badly wants green; and any trade where low-value chips are exchanged for higher-value ones from a player’s own perspective.

Strategically, proposers can exploit their on-screen surplus calculator to avoid self-harming deals. However, because they don’t know others’ values, they must infer them from acceptance behavior. Early in the game, players are likely to make exploratory offers with modest surplus for themselves that might also look appealing to at least one counterpart, learning who is more willing to trade which color. As the three rounds progress, players update their beliefs: frequent eagerness to offload a color suggests that color is low-value for that player; reluctance to trade suggests it is high-value. The random recipient rule when multiple accept introduces further uncertainty: a proposer cannot target a specific partner, so they must design offers that are safe and profitable regardless of which accepting player is matched. By the final round, players often make more aggressive, one-sided proposals to lock in last gains, especially those who see clear arbitrage opportunities between a low-value holder and a high-value seeker of a chip color.

In this forecast, trades are chosen to be consistent with each player seeking positive surplus on every accepted trade, limited learning over a single short game, and boundedly rational attempts to piece together others’ preferences from limited evidence.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of the game, all players hold 10 of each chip and have no direct information about others’ valuations. Proposals in this round are likely to be exploratory, with modestly favorable terms for the proposer but not extremely one-sided, in hopes of eliciting at least one acceptance and revealing who is willing to trade which chip. Because everyone is still uncertain, some profitable trades may be passed up due to caution or misjudgment about how others value colors.

After round 0, some information has been revealed: octopus showed a willingness to give up blue for green, and ram revealed a strong willingness to accept that deal, which indirectly signals that blue is quite valuable to ram and relatively cheap to octopus. Lion observed blue moving from octopus to ram at a fairly generous rate to ram, so lion may infer that octopus doesn’t like blue and that ram values it highly. Proposals in this round are more targeted: octopus is likely to keep selling blue for green, ram will seek further blue acquisitions or try to offload red, and lion may tentatively test whether they can buy green from octopus or sell red/green to ram for blue while still securing positive surplus.

Going into the final round, patterns are clearer. Octopus has repeatedly shown a strong desire for green over blue and a willingness to give up blue cheaply; ram has eagerly accumulated blue using both green and red, and has even sold red to obtain green, highlighting ram’s strong blue preference and relatively low red value. Lion has been more cautious, with no accepted trades so far, and faces limited remaining opportunities as the chip distributions have shifted. In this last round, players are likely to make more aggressive, immediate-surplus-maximizing proposals: octopus may try one last blue-for-green trade, ram might still attempt to exchange low-value red for green or even test whether lion will sell blue, and lion may probe for a safe, small surplus deal if any obvious arbitrage remains.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game features common-value green chips and privately valued red and blue chips. All players start with 10 of each chip type. Green chips are worth $0.50 to everyone, so trades in green mostly reallocate a neutral benchmark asset. Red and blue chips are where gains from trade arise: buffalo values red highly ($0.90) and blue very low ($0.10); duck values blue highly ($0.90) and red low ($0.40); cat sits in between with moderate blue ($0.20) and low red ($0.40). From an omniscient perspective, efficient trades convert low-value chips into high-value chips in others' hands: buffalo should want to send blue away in exchange for red or green, duck should want to send red or green away in exchange for blue, and cat is comparatively weakly motivated to trade because its marginal values are closer together and often below the other players' peak values. However, each participant only knows their own private values and may only infer others' values from observed behavior. Proposers see their own net change for any draft trade and aim for strictly positive gains. Responders see their own net gain/loss for the posted trade. A trade only occurs if at least one responder obtains a (weakly) positive payoff and chooses to accept; if both would gain they risk random assignment if both accept. Strategically, proposers will often post deals that are good for themselves and plausibly slightly good for at least one partner, without making it obviously too generous. Responders will accept any trade that strictly increases their own payoff and decline any trade that is clearly negative, with some caution if the gain is very small or if they anticipate better future deals. Early in the game, players experiment to learn about others' preferences; by later rounds they adjust proposals based on observed accept/decline patterns, though substantial uncertainty remains about exact valuations. The forecast below assumes moderately rational, self-interested behavior, simple incremental proposals, and a few partially successful trades that exploit the strong buffalo–duck complementarity (buffalo wants red; duck wants blue) while cat plays more selectively.

rounds.round_explanation: In the first round, players are effectively probing. None of them knows the others' values, so proposals are likely to be small, conservative trades designed to test interest without giving away too much. Buffalo will tentatively try to turn low-value blue into higher-value red or green, duck will seek blue in exchange for red or green, and cat will cautiously experiment, possibly trying to swap low-value red into green or blue. With no history yet, acceptances hinge purely on each responder’s private calculator; some offers that look good to the proposer may be bad for both responders and get rejected. We forecast one early failed probe, followed by a mutually beneficial trade between buffalo and duck that begins to reveal their opposing preferences, and then a modest cat–duck interaction as cat tests a small beneficial exchange.

By round 1, buffalo and duck have successfully traded once and each infers that the other is happy to move green for blue in opposite directions, hinting at complementary valuations. Buffalo now has slightly fewer blue and more green, duck the reverse. Cat has seen two declined attempts and may infer that the others are picky about green–red terms. This leads to more targeted proposals: buffalo pushes harder to obtain red (which is very valuable to buffalo) using low-value blue; duck continues to solicit blue using relatively cheap red; and cat reframes offers, trying to exploit at least one of the emerging buffalo–duck patterns while maintaining its own gains. We forecast that a buffalo–duck red–blue trade now succeeds, and cat manages a small beneficial trade with duck while still failing to interest buffalo.

In the final round, players have limited time to improve positions. Buffalo has already converted some blue into red and green and will selectively accept any last trades that increase value without giving up scarce red. Duck has accumulated more blue and given up some red and green; it is still eager to gain blue but must be careful not to overpay. Cat has made only one small profitable trade and may seek one more increment of surplus, but is unlikely to accept any move that looks even slightly negative. With these endgame incentives, proposers push slightly more aggressive but still plausible offers. Given the private calculators, only trades that are strictly positive to both sides occur; unattractive offers are quickly declined. We forecast one final modest buffalo–duck trade; cat’s last proposals fail because they would cost the responders, and the game ends with buffalo and duck having exploited most of their comparative advantage while cat remains near its initial allocation with a small gain.

parsed_target.game_explanation:
This three-player bargaining game features asymmetric private values on red and blue chips and a common value on green chips. All players start with a symmetric 10–10–10 endowment across RED, BLUE, and GREEN, but value those chips very differently. Butterfly has low value for BLUE (0.10) and moderate for RED (0.30); Kangaroo has high value for RED (0.70) and somewhat higher-than-butterfly value for BLUE (0.20); Squid has very high value for BLUE (0.80) and mid-range RED (0.40). All value GREEN equally at 0.50. This structure makes BLUE systematically most valuable to Squid, RED most valuable to Kangaroo, and BLUE still relatively more valuable to Squid than to Butterfly. Efficient trade patterns would move BLUE from Butterfly and somewhat from Kangaroo toward Squid, and move RED from Butterfly (and to a lesser extent from Squid) toward Kangaroo, with GREEN potentially used as a common currency to bridge conflicting beliefs about others’ valuations.

However, each player knows only their own value vector, so they must infer others’ preferences from offers and acceptances. The UI ensures that any player will accept only proposals that provide a nonnegative surplus in their own terms, so irrational losses are unlikely. The random recipient rule when multiple players accept adds further uncertainty, weakening targeted bilateral deals: proposers cannot safely design a trade “for” a specific partner unless they are confident the other potential recipient will decline. Strategic behavior thus involves: (1) proposing trades that are strictly profitable for the sender, (2) constructing terms that are likely to be (weakly) attractive to at least one counterpart, yet (3) if possible, screening out unwanted recipients by making the deal less attractive for them.

Across three rounds (nine proposal turns), one expects early offers to be somewhat exploratory: small, positive-surplus-for-sender offers using GREEN (the common-value chip) as a neutral medium, or asking for BLUE/RED in exchange for GREEN to test how strongly others respond. Over time, players can update beliefs based on patterns of acceptance: Squid’s willingness to give up RED or GREEN to buy BLUE will signal high BLUE value; Kangaroo’s willingness to pay GREEN or BLUE for RED will signal high RED value; Butterfly may gravitate toward selling BLUE and possibly RED if they can obtain sufficient GREEN or higher-valued chips. The short horizon (three rounds) constrains how much learning can occur, so many proposals will be conservative and slightly skewed toward the proposer, occasionally leading to no-acceptance outcomes when the implied gain for others is too small or negative. Simultaneous responses and random assignment in multi-accept situations mean some accepted trades will look more like opportunistic grabs of obvious gains rather than finely tuned bilateral bargains.

rounds.round_explanation: In round 0, no one has observed any prior play, so beliefs about others’ values are very noisy. Proposers are likely to test simple, one-color-for-one-color trades with favorable terms for themselves, especially using GREEN as a safe medium since all know the public range for green is fixed and intermediate. Butterfly moves first and is especially inclined to shed BLUE (low value to her) in exchange for either RED or GREEN, but she must guess whether others see the same. Kangaroo, with strong RED preferences, will likely try to acquire RED cheaply while offloading some BLUE or possibly GREEN, probing whether others undervalue RED. Squid, knowing BLUE is very valuable to him, will seek to buy BLUE or protect his own BLUE while possibly offering RED or GREEN. In this first round, many offers may either be declined for offering insufficient surplus or accepted if they accidentally line up with others’ private values, catalyzing early learning.

By round 1, players have observed that Squid was eager to accept BLUE-for-GREEN trades (from Butterfly’s first move) and that Kangaroo accepted a BLUE-for-GREEN trade proposed by Squid. This suggests to Butterfly that both others may value BLUE more than GREEN, confirming her suspicion that BLUE is one of her least valuable chips. Kangaroo infers that Squid is willing to pay GREEN for BLUE, hinting at a high BLUE value; Squid sees that Butterfly was willing to part with BLUE cheaply and that Kangaroo was willing to sell BLUE for GREEN, reinforcing his belief that he can profitably acquire BLUE from either player. In this round, Butterfly is likely to push further into offloading BLUE, possibly at slightly better terms for the counterpart to ensure acceptance. Kangaroo will still pursue RED but may be more cautious about paying too much GREEN now that his GREEN stock is lower. Squid, having gained some BLUE, may still be willing to trade RED or GREEN to consolidate BLUE, but will watch his GREEN more carefully.

Entering the final round, substantial information has been revealed through patterns of trade: both Butterfly and Kangaroo have repeatedly sold BLUE for GREEN, while Squid has been a consistent buyer of BLUE and, once, a seller of BLUE-to-RED with Kangaroo. This triangulation implies to attentive players that Squid’s BLUE value is high and Butterfly’s is low; Kangaroo appears intermediate but increasingly focused on RED. Butterfly now holds relatively few BLUE chips and more GREEN, having converted low-value BLUE into higher-value GREEN; she has less scope for further BLUE sales and may try a modest RED-for-GREEN trade if she believes others might value RED more. Kangaroo, with increased BLUE and some extra GREEN from earlier trades, still wants RED but must avoid overpaying in the last few turns. Squid, with abundant BLUE already, is closer to his ideal portfolio and may be selective, only accepting strongly profitable deals. Because the game ends after this round, all players have weaker incentives to leave potential gains unrealized, but the narrow remaining margins and inventory constraints still make some proposals unattractive, so not all final offers will clear.

parsed_target.game_explanation:
In this three-player bargaining game, each participant starts with an equal endowment of 10 red, 10 blue, and 10 green chips but values red and blue differently. Green chips are known to be worth $0.50 to everyone and are common-value. Red and blue ranges are publicly known, but exact values are private. Thus, players know that others may value a given color more or less than they do, but they do not know the exact valuations. This creates tension between exploiting perceived comparative advantages and avoiding trades that might leave money on the table if a partner is actually less mismatched in values than expected.

The private values imply clear comparative advantages:
- bat-pink-1751 (Bat) values BLUE the most ($0.60), GREEN mid ($0.50), and RED the least ($0.20). Bat wants to trade away RED in exchange for BLUE or GREEN.
- fox-green-2854 (Fox) has the strongest preference for BLUE ($1.00), then RED ($0.60), then GREEN ($0.50). Fox most wants BLUE, and is most willing to part with GREEN and, to a lesser extent, RED.
- frog-blue-6296 (Frog) values RED the most ($0.70), then GREEN ($0.50), and BLUE the least ($0.30). Frog wants to acquire RED, especially by trading away BLUE.

From an omniscient perspective that knows the true values, the most efficient trades are: Frog gives BLUE to Fox (who values BLUE at $1.00) in exchange for RED (which Frog values at $0.70 and Fox at $0.60), and Bat gives RED to either Fox or Frog in exchange for BLUE (because Bat values BLUE more than RED). These potential trades create positive surplus for both sides if terms are chosen within a sensible band. However, because each player only sees their own values, they will anchor on their own valuation differences and use the surplus calculator to avoid personal losses. Proposers will experiment with offers that give the other side some gain while preserving positive surplus for themselves, but they cannot tailor offers perfectly without knowing others’ exact values.

Strategically, all players know that green is a safe, symmetric currency at $0.50: they may use it as a medium for small, low-risk trades. Red and blue, however, are where private-value advantages lie; players will probe how willing others are to sell blue or red by making offers that are clearly good for themselves, but not obviously terrible for a wide range of opponent valuations. Since each turn executes immediately if accepted, early trades reveal information about counterpart willingness to swap specific colors but not precise values. Later rounds may feature more targeted proposals if earlier acceptance patterns suggest who is relatively willing to part with certain colors. The randomization when multiple responders accept adds additional uncertainty: a proposer cannot choose which of the two responders they will actually trade with, so safe, one-sided offers that only one partner is likely to accept are more attractive than balanced offers that could appeal to both.

Overall, we should see modest, value-driven trades that exploit clear comparative advantages: Bat offering RED for BLUE or GREEN, Fox offering GREEN (and sometimes RED) for BLUE, and Frog offering BLUE for RED. Players will avoid obviously negative trades according to their calculators, leading to some declined offers where proposers are too aggressive. As the game unfolds over three rounds, slight updating about others’ preferences can occur, but the game is short enough that players mainly rely on their own valuations and coarse expectations about others instead of fully learning the entire value structure.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of round 0, all players have symmetric holdings (10 of each color) but only know their own values. They expect that others differ in how they rank red and blue within the known $0.10–$1.00 range. Proposers are likely to start with relatively conservative, surplus-positive offers that exploit their own comparative advantages without risking obvious losses. Since no trade history exists yet, each player’s first turn serves both to seek value and to probe how responsive the others are to particular color trades.

By round 1, two informative trades have occurred. Bat successfully traded away cheap red for more valuable blue, revealing an apparent willingness by Frog to part with blue. Frog then exchanged low-valued blue for high-valued red from Fox, indicating Fox is willing to give up some red when compensated in blue. Everyone now has shifted holdings and some evidence that both Fox and Frog are open to blue–red trades at favorable rates. Proposers can refine their offers, pushing a bit harder on these observed comparative advantages while still ensuring the calculator shows a personal gain.

Going into the final round, several successful trades have further specialized holdings: Bat has accumulated more blue and some green at the expense of red, Fox has concentrated somewhat in blue and red while shedding green, and Frog has bolstered red holdings while reducing blue. Players now have clearer, though still imperfect, impressions of each other’s willingness to swap specific colors. With only three turns left, they are incentivized to make high-value but still safe offers that move them closer to their ideal mix without risking negative surplus on the final trades.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players start symmetric in chip counts (10 of each color) but not in how they value red and blue. Dog and Turtle share identical values (BLUE=$1, RED=$0.50, GREEN=$0.50), so they both strongly prefer blue and are relatively indifferent between red and green. Parrot values RED=$0.60, BLUE=$0.30, GREEN=$0.50, so Parrot is the only player who likes red more than blue and likes green more than blue as well. From a surplus perspective, the natural efficiency pattern is to move blue chips toward Dog and Turtle and move red chips toward Parrot. However, each participant only knows their own valuations, so early in the game they must infer others’ preferences from offers and responses. Because accept/decline decisions are based on a clear on-screen calculator, players rarely accept trades that give them negative surplus. Dog and Turtle have parallel incentives and also compete over Parrot’s willingness to give up red; that competition should push them toward offering Parrot slightly favorable exchanges when trying to buy red. Parrot, by contrast, will test the market by first trying to get blue cheaply or even for free, and later may realize that Dog and Turtle value blue more and adjust by offering to swap red for blue at ratios that leave everyone weakly better off. Uncertainty is primarily about how much each player can extract as a share of the available surplus, not about whether positive-surplus trades exist. Because the proposer schedule is fixed and short (three rounds, each player proposes once per round), proposals later in the game are increasingly shaped by revealed acceptances and rejections in earlier turns, with players refining their beliefs (for example, that others like blue a lot or dislike red) and adjusting the price ratios accordingly.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of Round 0, no one has seen any trading behavior yet, so each player is probing. Dog and Turtle know they like blue a lot, but they are unsure which colors others value. Parrot knows blue is relatively weak and red is relatively strong for them, but does not know that Dog and Turtle share a strong preference for blue. The most likely early dynamics are small, conservative offers that test willingness to trade red for blue or green and try to generate strictly positive surplus for the proposer without obviously making the recipient worse off. Rejections in this round are especially informative and will shape beliefs for Rounds 1 and 2.

After Round 0, two key pieces of information are revealed through behavior. First, Dog learned that Parrot was willing to give up two highly valuable blue chips in exchange for three red chips, which are lower value for Dog but higher value for Parrot; this hints to Dog that Parrot might like red more than blue. Second, Parrot’s successful sale of a blue chip to Turtle for two green chips reveals that Turtle is willing to trade off blue even for a modest gain in green, consistent with Turtle having strong blue value but also some flexibility. The rejected 1-for-1 red–blue swap from Turtle suggests that neither Dog nor Parrot finds a symmetric red–blue trade attractive at par. Going into Round 1, players update: Dog and Turtle will be more confident that Parrot values red more and blue less; Parrot will suspect that Dog and Turtle value blue strongly and green moderately, and will likely adjust offers to extract better terms on red-for-blue trades.

Going into the final round, the pattern is clearer. Dog has repeatedly bought blue from Parrot using red, and Parrot has repeatedly been willing to give up blue when paid in red or green, confirming Parrot’s relatively low valuation of blue and strong valuation of red. Turtle has also now managed to buy red from Parrot with blue, so Parrot understands that both Dog and Turtle are keen buyers of red and sellers of blue. This reduces uncertainty about relative valuations and makes the remaining bargaining mainly about how to split surplus. Because it is the last round, players have no reason to hold chips for future bargaining; any strictly positive-surplus trade that is available and recognized is likely to be executed, although strategic mispricing or remaining misperceptions can still cause some offers to be rejected. We should see final attempts by Dog and Turtle to buy any remaining cheap blue from Parrot and by Parrot to harvest value by converting weak blue into stronger red or green, subject to the constraint that each party still refuses net-negative trades according to the calculator.

parsed_target.game_explanation: In this three-player chip bargaining game, all players start with the same inventory (10 RED, 10 BLUE, 10 GREEN), but have slightly different private values for red chips. GREEN chips are common-value at $0.50 each for everyone, BLUE are low-value at $0.10 for everyone, and RED are worth $0.30 to Badger and Peacock but $0.50 to Unicorn. This makes Unicorn the high-RED-type player and the natural buyer of red chips; Badger and Peacock are relatively indifferent between red and green (both at $0.30 and $0.50), but they strictly prefer green to blue and are willing sellers of blue. Because blue is the weakest chip for all players, everyone will try to trade away blue for higher-value colors when possible. From the analyst’s perspective, socially efficient trades move RED chips from Badger/Peacock to Unicorn and BLUE chips away from everyone toward green or red. However, uncertainty and asymmetric information drive the bargaining. Each player only sees their own values and must infer others’ preferences from proposals and acceptances. Unicorn knows that trading blue ($0.10) for red ($0.30 or $0.50 depending on belief) or green ($0.50) is profitable, but cannot be sure how much surplus Badger and Peacock get from red or green. Badger and Peacock each face the same symmetric situation and will test offers that convert their low-value blue into green or red. They may slowly reveal their rough valuation profile (for instance, they likely do not sacrifice green to buy blue), but will be cautious giving away too many reds because those might be valuable to others. With three rounds and three turns per round, initial proposals are often exploratory, aiming for small positive expected gains and checking whether others are willing to accept. Over time, once it becomes clearer that red is probably more valuable to Unicorn, Badger and Peacock may mark up their ask in red-for-blue or green-for-red trades. Because acceptances are private and simultaneous and any multiple acceptances are resolved by randomization, senders must design offers attractive enough that at least one counterpart sees a positive surplus yet not so generous that they overpay. This leads to modest, incremental trades centered on BLUE-for-RED or BLUE-for-GREEN swaps, with occasional RED-for-GREEN trades between Badger/Peacock and Unicorn. The end-state is likely one where Unicorn has accumulated additional red chips at the cost of blue and possibly some green, while the other two players have slightly increased their green and/or blue holdings, reflecting mild but not full convergence to the efficient allocation.

rounds.round_explanation: In round 0, players are feeling out each other’s values with relatively small, conservative trades. No one yet knows that Unicorn values red more, so Badger and Peacock will tend to offer modest blue-for-green or blue-for-red trades that safely improve their own payoff if accepted. Unicorn will try cheap ways to acquire red or green using blue, but will avoid overpaying early. Expect low-quantity offers and some declined trades as players calibrate what others are willing to accept.

By round 1, players have observed that blue is relatively undesirable for everyone and that small BLUE-for-GREEN or BLUE-for-RED trades can clear. Badger has just sold red to Unicorn, subtly revealing that they do not treat red as especially precious. Unicorn now has evidence that others may be willing to part with red at modest prices. Expect slightly more targeted attempts by Unicorn to buy red from both partners, and some counter-moves by Badger and Peacock to acquire green using their remaining blue. The bargaining remains cautious, but with clearer directional beliefs.

In the final round, inventories have shifted: Unicorn has acquired extra red but given up some blue and green; Badger and Peacock have slightly upgraded blue into green or red. With no future rounds, players are more willing to propose slightly more aggressive trades that still guarantee them a small gain, since they cannot save bargaining power for later. Unicorn may attempt a last push to buy red using remaining blue, while Badger and Peacock attempt final blue-for-green upgrades. Nonetheless, players avoid value-destroying trades (like buying blue with green) because accept/decline decisions are made with full surplus information.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player game features common-value green chips and privately valued red and blue chips. All players start with 10 of each chip type; they know only their own dollar values but can infer relative preferences over time from observed proposals and acceptances. Dog and Owl both value blue chips highly ($1.00) and red moderately ($0.80 and $0.30), while Mouse values blue modestly ($0.60) and red very low ($0.20). Green chips are worth $0.50 to everyone. As proposers, players see a surplus calculator that reveals whether a given trade is profitable in their own units; responders see the same for their side. Because acceptance decisions are simultaneous and recipients are randomly selected if multiple players accept, proposers must consider that any attractive deal to more than one counterpart might execute with the less-preferred partner, potentially changing the future bargaining landscape. Rational players try to improve their own portfolio value in each turn, but they also face uncertainty about others' valuations and future trading opportunities. The likely pattern is that Dog and Owl compete over blue chips while trying to shed less valuable colors, and Mouse attempts to sell red (and sometimes blue) to those who value them more, in exchange for green or other colors that are relatively more valuable to Mouse. Early rounds serve partly as information revelation: who eagerly accepts what helps each player infer others’ tastes. Over the three rounds, we should see a few mutually beneficial trades that move chips from low-value holders to high-value holders, with some offers rejected when they leave the responder worse off or when a responder strategically waits for a better deal.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start, all players have symmetric chip counts but asymmetric private values. No one yet knows others’ valuations, so round 0 is exploratory. Dog, as a strong blue demander with decent red, is likely to probe for blue from someone who appears willing to part with it, offering red or green. Mouse, who has the lowest red value, is naturally inclined to test trades that dump red in exchange for colors that are relatively more useful, especially green and possibly blue if they seem cheap to others. Owl, sharing Dog’s high blue valuation but weaker red value than Dog, may probe for additional blue or green while offering some of Owl’s relatively less-valued red. Responses in this round begin to reveal who treats which chip types as expendable or precious.

By round 1, Dog has revealed some willingness to acquire red from Mouse (by accepting red for green in round 0), suggesting to others that Dog does not consider red extremely weak. Mouse has signaled that red is relatively cheap for Mouse and that green is desirable. Owl tentatively showed an interest in blue but did not find willing partners for a low-red offer. Now players update beliefs: Mouse may continue to offload red against green but must be careful not to overpay; Dog can try to deepen the red-for-green or red-for-blue pattern if profitable; Owl, seeing Dog’s interest in green and red, may seek green while selling red, or look for slightly better terms on blue. Trades that clearly hurt responders, given their own values, are unlikely to be accepted, so proposers must refine terms closer to mutual gain.

In the final round, chip holdings and revealed preferences are clearer: Dog has shown strong interest in both red and blue and willingness to part with green; Mouse has repeatedly traded away red for green, indicating that Mouse’s valuation gap between red and green is substantial; Owl is willing to swap some red for green and has previously accepted green-for-blue from Dog, indicating that green is also useful to Owl. With limited time left, players focus on any remaining obvious gains from trade. Dog may try one last time to convert green into valuable blue while still leaving responders weakly better off; Mouse may attempt a small final red-for-green improvement; Owl may look for marginal improvements in the blue/red mix but faces tighter constraints as others preserve their remaining surplus. Risk of rejection is higher because responders know this is the last chance and will only accept clearly profitable offers.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This is a three-player alternating-offer bargaining game with privately known values for red and blue chips and common value for green chips. Everyone starts with a symmetric endowment of 10 per color, but their dollar payoffs per chip differ. Green chips are worth $0.50 to all, so they are neutral and mainly serve as a convenient, non-controversial medium of exchange. The private values create hidden gains from trade: alligator values red the most of the three except for rabbit’s very high red value, buffalo likes red more than blue but less than the others, and rabbit has an especially strong preference for red over blue. Because each player only sees their own values, they know that trading away low-value colors for higher-value colors is good for them, but they are uncertain how much others value each color and thus how far they can push the terms of trade. The surplus calculator makes sure that any rational acceptance decision is driven by the player’s own gains in that moment, so dominated deals are rarely accepted. The main strategic tension is between proposing aggressive, high-margin trades that might be declined and more balanced, obviously mutually beneficial deals that are more likely to be accepted. With random partner selection when multiple players accept, proposers cannot fully target a specific opponent; they must offer terms that are attractive to at least one counterpart and acceptable if either counterpart ends up trading. Over three rounds with fixed proposer order, players update inferentially from observed acceptances and rejections about how others value colors, but in this one-shot game they are unlikely to perfectly coordinate on full efficiency. The likely pattern is a mix of clearly profitable cross-color trades (especially exchanging green or blue for red) and some declined attempts when proposers ask for too favorable a rate or unintentionally push offers that yield losses for both potential recipients.

rounds.round_explanation: In the first round, all players start with symmetric holdings and no information about others’ private values beyond public ranges. They will tend to make simple, easy-to-evaluate proposals using green chips as a neutral numeraire. Alligator, valuing red somewhat more than blue and equal to green, is inclined to try buying red with green at a favorable but not extreme rate. Buffalo, who values red more than blue but not dramatically, will likely test trading green for red as well, albeit at modest terms. Rabbit, who has a very high value for red, will already be keen to acquire red, probably by giving up blue, which they value least. Most first-round behavior is exploratory: players float terms that make sense from their own perspective and observe what gets accepted to infer how others might value colors.

By the second round, some information has been revealed: buffalo has shown willingness to trade red for blue and then to accept a slightly more favorable blue-for-red deal with rabbit. Rabbit now holds more red and fewer blue chips, reinforcing their comparative advantage in red consumption. Alligator has already improved their position in red relative to green. Given these adjustments, rabbit has even stronger incentives to keep accumulating red and dumping blue, while buffalo may become more cautious about further losing red unless terms are clearly profitable. Alligator is likely to test smaller or more targeted trades, possibly trying to exploit any perceived undervaluation of red or overvaluation of blue by others. Players now refine their offers, trying to capture surplus while still offering clearly beneficial deals to at least one partner.

Entering the final round, players understand that no further bargaining is possible beyond these last three offers, so they focus on clearly value-improving trades and avoid any marginal or risky ones. Alligator and rabbit both have accumulated extra red relative to their starting positions, while buffalo has reduced red holdings and is more value-sensitive about parting with any remaining high-value chips. Given the time pressure, proposers tend to offer deals that are obviously positive for at least one counterpart, often centered on exchanging relatively low-value blue chips for higher-value red or neutral green. Because each player now has some inferred beliefs about who values what, they may direct offers that are more likely to be taken by the counterpart with the complementary valuation, while still being acceptable if either accepts due to random partner selection. The last moves thus emphasize simple, clearly profitable exchanges and avoid speculative or opaque trades.
